title: Review follow-up: bootstrap trust-boundary enforcement and console reload contract
description:
Context
A fresh-eyes post-implementation review of the ids_ml_new-fuf5 wave found two concrete follow-up problems that must now be tracked as a self-contained execution graph:

1. Bootstrap trust-boundary hardening is incomplete in the actual `bootstrap` path. `ids.ops.same_host_stack.run_stack_bootstrap()` executes mutating module commands before `validate_stack_preflight()` runs, and the final `bootstrap_ready` result does not incorporate a degraded preflight outcome.
2. The canonical console server entrypoint exposes `--reload`, but `ids.console.server.run_server()` passes an already-instantiated FastAPI app object into `uvicorn.run(..., reload=True)`, which Uvicorn does not support.

Goal
Close both review findings without needing to reopen the original markdown planning artifacts. This epic is the source of truth for the required implementation, test, and verification work.

Execution model
- Workstream A is a blocking P1 security/ops fix for bootstrap trust-boundary enforcement.
- Workstream B is a P2 console/runtime fix for the canonical reload contract.
- Each workstream includes design/decision capture, implementation, regression coverage, and final targeted verification.

Constraints
- Canonical `ids/*` modules remain the primary product surface.
- Compatibility wrappers remain supported surfaces and must not drift from canonical behavior.
- Regression coverage must prove the fixed behavior through real runtime seams, not only via shallow monkeypatches.
- This epic traces back to the closed source review epic `ids_ml_new-fuf5`, but it is self-contained and does not require the old markdown review notes.
acceptance_criteria:
Checklist
- Both review root issues under this epic are resolved and closed.
- Bootstrap fails closed before any untrusted mutating module command or service start when trust-boundary validation is degraded.
- The canonical console server either supports reload through a valid Uvicorn contract or no longer advertises an unsupported reload surface.
- Targeted tests/proofs exist for both workstreams and are named directly in the closing verification notes.
- The bead graph is self-contained enough that implementation can proceed without consulting the original review markdown.
close_reason: Both review root issues are resolved: bootstrap trust-boundary enforcement and console reload contract fixes are implemented, regression-covered, and verified with targeted pytest proof surfaces.

title: Resolve Review P1: bootstrap runs untrusted modules before trust-boundary preflight
description:
Problem
The bootstrap path originally appended `stack_preflight` after mutating bundle/operator commands and service-start work. That meant module trust-boundary validation did not actually defend the bootstrap mutation path.

Subtler failure discovered during execution
A naive repair that simply ran the full `validate_stack_preflight()` payload up front caused bootstrap to fail on expected pre-bootstrap conditions such as a missing activation record or operator database. The real contract had to distinguish:
- trust-boundary prerequisites that must block bootstrap immediately
- broader runtime-readiness checks that may legitimately be degraded before bootstrap has created state

Resolved contract
- Bootstrap always records `stack_preflight` first.
- Bootstrap blocks immediately when trust-boundary/module-containment gating is not satisfied.
- Bootstrap does not treat expected pre-bootstrap runtime gaps as fatal when the trust gate itself is ready.
- Hostile module injection still fails closed before verify/promote/migrate/bootstrap-admin or service starts.
design:
Implementation summary
- `validate_stack_preflight()` now returns both broad `ready` and narrower `bootstrap_gate_ready` semantics.
- `bootstrap_gate_ready` captures the bootstrap-safe trust gate: host layout/module containment checks plus the ability to load operator config.
- `run_stack_bootstrap()` uses the trust gate for fail-closed control flow while preserving the broader degraded diagnostics in the payload.
- Internal tests prove preflight-first ordering, degraded trust-gate stop behavior, and degraded post-start status/smoke behavior.
- Installed proofs prove both a successful bootstrap path and a hostile-module bootstrap block path through the real packaged CLI seam.
acceptance_criteria:
Checklist
- Bootstrap validates trust boundaries before any mutating module command or service start.
- Degraded trust-gate state prevents verify/promote/migrate/bootstrap-admin and prevents service starts.
- Expected pre-bootstrap readiness gaps remain visible in diagnostics without incorrectly blocking a legitimate bootstrap.
- The behavior is proven by both internal orchestration tests and repo-installable bootstrap proofs.
notes:
Final proof commands
- `python -m pytest tests/ops/test_ids_same_host_stack_manage.py -q`
- `python -m pytest tests/ops/test_ids_repo_installable_bootstrap_proof.py -q`

Key scenarios proven
- hostile trust-boundary failure blocks mutation before side effects
- healthy trust gate allows bootstrap even when activation/database do not exist yet
- degraded post-start status/smoke still yields degraded bootstrap outcome
close_reason: Bootstrap now gates on trust-boundary readiness before mutation/service start, fails closed on degraded trust checks, and is proven by passing internal and installed-command regressions.

title: Define fail-closed bootstrap trust-boundary contract and allowed preflight ordering
description:
Purpose
Define the exact bootstrap trust-boundary contract before any code reordering so later beads had an unambiguous target.

Contract that was locked by this bead
- `stack_preflight` must be the first reported bootstrap step.
- The bootstrap control gate is not identical to the full runtime-readiness result.
- The control gate covers only the prerequisites that make bootstrap mutation safe: repo-root/module-containment checks, executable/env-path validity, and operator-config loadability.
- Broader readiness gaps such as missing activation records, missing operator databases, or missing live-sensor outputs can be diagnostically degraded before bootstrap and must not automatically block a legitimate bootstrap run.
- When the control gate fails, bootstrap must return a degraded payload containing only the `stack_preflight` step and must not run mutation or service-start work.
design:
Why this bead existed separately
Without this explicit contract, the implementation could not distinguish between:
- genuine security/trust-boundary failures that must fail closed immediately
- expected pre-bootstrap emptiness that bootstrap itself is supposed to repair

This bead therefore defined the semantic split later implemented as `bootstrap_gate_ready` versus broad `ready`.
acceptance_criteria:
Checklist
- The graph body explicitly states what blocks bootstrap and what is merely diagnostic before bootstrap runs.
- The contract explains why a full `ready` check is too broad for bootstrap gating.
- Later implementation and test beads can be judged directly against this contract without outside planning notes.
notes:
Downstream effect
This contract directly shaped:
- `.1.2` ordering changes
- `.1.3` fail-closed gating semantics
- `.1.4` internal regression coverage
- `.1.5` installed hostile-bootstrap proof
close_reason: Defined the bootstrap trust-boundary contract in code/tests, split bootstrap gate from overall readiness, and verified the P1 proof surfaces green.

title: Move stack_preflight ahead of module mutations in run_stack_bootstrap
description:
Objective
Move the trust-boundary preflight step ahead of every bootstrap action that could execute attacker-controlled or out-of-bound module code.

Concrete code path guarded by this bead
- candidate bundle verification
- candidate bundle promotion
- operator console migration
- bootstrap-admin user creation
- all later service-start commands

Final implementation outcome
`run_stack_bootstrap()` now evaluates preflight first, records the `stack_preflight` step first in the payload, and leaves no hidden path where mutation executes before the trust gate.
design:
Why this was separate from fail-closed semantics
Reordering alone was necessary but not sufficient. The code also needed `.1.3` to ensure the result stayed degraded when the trust gate failed.

Readable-step requirement preserved
The payload still exposes an operator-readable ordered `steps` list rather than hiding preflight in a side helper.
acceptance_criteria:
Checklist
- `stack_preflight` is evaluated before verify/promote/migrate/bootstrap-admin.
- No `systemctl start` happens before the trust gate passes.
- The returned bootstrap step list shows the new order directly.
notes:
Primary proof points
- step-order assertions in `tests/ops/test_ids_same_host_stack_manage.py`
- happy-path installed bootstrap proof ordering assertions in `tests/ops/test_ids_repo_installable_bootstrap_proof.py`
close_reason: stack_preflight now executes before bundle/operator mutations and service starts, and the bootstrap payload reflects the new ordering.

title: Gate bootstrap result and service start on preflight readiness
description:
Objective
Make bootstrap fail closed when the trust gate is degraded while preserving operator-visible diagnostic context.

Resolved behavior
- If `bootstrap_gate_ready` is false, bootstrap returns immediately with `bootstrap_ready=false`, top-level `status=degraded`, and only the `stack_preflight` step recorded.
- The returned payload retains the degraded preflight diagnosis so operators and tests can see why bootstrap was blocked.
- If the trust gate is ready but later post-start checks degrade, bootstrap still returns degraded after the later `status`/`smoke` stages.
design:
Key semantic distinction
- `bootstrap_gate_ready`: controls whether bootstrap may mutate or start services at all
- `ready`: broader runtime readiness, useful for diagnostics and standalone preflight, but too broad to control bootstrap entry

This split was the critical repair that allowed both safety and a functioning bootstrap workflow.
acceptance_criteria:
Checklist
- Degraded trust-gate state prevents later mutation/service-start work.
- The top-level bootstrap payload fails closed on trust-gate degradation.
- Diagnostic preflight context is preserved in the returned payload.
- Degraded post-start verification still produces degraded bootstrap output after legitimate bootstrap work has run.
notes:
Primary proof points
- `test_run_stack_bootstrap_bootstrap_or_preflight_stops_on_degraded_preflight`
- `test_run_stack_bootstrap_bootstrap_or_preflight_reports_degraded_post_start_verification`
- CLI degraded exit semantics proven in `test_manage_main_bootstrap_returns_degraded_exit_code`
close_reason: Bootstrap now fails closed on trust-boundary gate failure via bootstrap_gate_ready while preserving diagnostic preflight context and degraded exit semantics.

title: Add unit and integration tests for preflight-before-mutation bootstrap gating
description:
Objective
Add focused internal regression coverage so the bootstrap trust-boundary repair cannot silently drift.

Coverage now contained in this bead
- payload step ordering proves preflight-first execution
- degraded trust-gate case proves no mutation or service-start commands run
- degraded bootstrap payload semantics are asserted directly
- CLI exit behavior still maps degraded bootstrap to exit code 2
- pre-bootstrap runtime-gap coverage proves broad readiness can be degraded while the bootstrap gate remains open
design:
Testing philosophy
This bead intentionally used observable command-runner side effects and payload assertions rather than implementation-detail mocks only. The tests are meant to fail if someone later collapses the trust-gate/readiness distinction or moves mutation back ahead of preflight.
acceptance_criteria:
Checklist
- Internal regression tests cover ordering, fail-closed trust-gate stop behavior, degraded payload semantics, and CLI degraded exit behavior.
- The tests distinguish expected pre-bootstrap gaps from actual trust-gate failures.
- The suite is strong enough to fail on the original review bug and on the over-broad repair that blocked healthy bootstrap.
notes:
Verified by
- `python -m pytest tests/ops/test_ids_same_host_stack_manage.py -q` -> passing after the final repair
close_reason: Added passing orchestration regressions for preflight-first ordering, degraded trust-gate stop behavior, payload semantics, and degraded CLI exit behavior in tests/ops/test_ids_same_host_stack_manage.py.

title: Extend repo-installable bootstrap proof to cover hostile module injection during bootstrap
description:
Objective
Prove the repaired trust-boundary behavior through the real installed `ids-stack bootstrap` seam, not only through internal unit-style orchestration tests.

Proof scenarios now covered
- successful installed bootstrap path where the trust gate is ready even though pre-bootstrap readiness is still degraded
- hostile shadow-module injection path where the trust gate is degraded and bootstrap must fail before mutation side effects

Side-effect discipline
The hostile proof checks that blocked bootstrap does not silently progress through the same activation/admin/service-start side effects as a successful path.
design:
Why this bead mattered
The original review finding was about the real bootstrap seam, not just a helper function. This bead made sure the repaired behavior survived packaging, editable-install bootstrap, wrapper/entrypoint execution, and real CLI argument flow.
acceptance_criteria:
Checklist
- There is a CLI-level proof that healthy trust-gated bootstrap succeeds even while pre-bootstrap readiness diagnostics are still degraded.
- There is a CLI-level proof that hostile module injection degrades the trust gate and blocks bootstrap before mutation side effects.
- The proof would fail if bootstrap again mutated state before the trust gate.
notes:
Verified by
- `python -m pytest tests/ops/test_ids_repo_installable_bootstrap_proof.py -q` -> passing after final trust-gate repair
- specific installed hostile-bootstrap scenario lives in `test_repo_installable_bootstrap_proof_blocks_hostile_module_before_mutation`
close_reason: Installed bootstrap proof now includes a hostile module-injection bootstrap scenario that blocks mutation and proves bootstrap_gate_ready false before side effects.

title: Run targeted verification matrix for bootstrap trust-boundary gating fix
description:
Objective
Close the P1 workstream with explicit targeted verification rather than assuming the fix is done because unit edits compile.

Verification scope
- The focused same-host stack manage test slice covering bootstrap ordering/gating.
- The repo-installable bootstrap proof slice covering hostile module injection during bootstrap.
- The trust-boundary/module-validation regressions already introduced in phase 5 that could be impacted by the reorder.

This bead exists so the final closure artifact names the exact proof commands/files that made the P1 finding safe to close.
acceptance_criteria:
Checklist
- The closing notes name the exact targeted pytest commands or test cases used for verification.
- The verification set covers both internal orchestration tests and installed-command proof.
- No P1 bootstrap trust-boundary gap remains after the verification set passes.
close_reason: Verified with python -m pytest tests/ops/test_ids_same_host_stack_manage.py -q and python -m pytest tests/ops/test_ids_repo_installable_bootstrap_proof.py -q; internal orchestration and installed bootstrap proof are both green.

title: Resolve Review P1: Bind bootstrap module execution to the validated trust boundary
description:
## Problem Statement

Bootstrap preflight now validates `model_manage_module`, `operator_manage_module`, and `operator_server_module` under a scrubbed trusted-root import check, but the privileged bootstrap actions still execute those modules later through plain `python -m ...` invocations. That splits approval from execution: preflight can approve one module origin while runtime execution resolves a different module from ambient import state. Because `verify`, `promote`, `migrate`, and `bootstrap-admin` run with privileged stack/bootstrap authority, this is a real trust-boundary bypass rather than a cosmetic drift.

## Evidence

**File:** `ids/ops/same_host_stack.py`
**Line(s):** around `_build_module_command`, `run_command`, and `run_stack_bootstrap`

- `_build_module_command()` constructs `[python, -m, <module>, ...]` commands.
- `run_command()` launches subprocesses without reusing the isolated interpreter contract or scrubbing inherited `PYTHON*` environment for these module invocations.
- `run_stack_bootstrap()` uses those commands for `verify`, `promote`, `migrate`, and `bootstrap-admin` after preflight has already approved the trusted module names.

**Why this is a problem:**
A hostile `cwd`, `PYTHONPATH`, `PYTHONHOME`, user-site package, or other import-path contamination can let preflight validate the trusted repo module and then cause bootstrap to execute a shadow module under the bootstrap user's privileges.

> KNOWN PATTERN: Matches 20260328-live-sensor-runtime-contracts.md and 20260331-repo-structure-wrapper-contracts.md.
> Previous occurrence: trust-boundary and containment failures where validation was separated from later authorization/execution.
> Past resolution: bind privileged execution to the same validated origin or execute under the same isolated/scrubbed contract used for validation.

## Proposed Solutions

### Option A — Recommended: execute modules under the same hardened interpreter contract
**Pros:** Preserves the module-based product surface while making runtime execution match preflight assumptions.
**Cons:** Requires threading isolated-env/cwd rules into the command runner and proof tests.
**Effort:** Medium

### Option B — bind execution to validated file origins instead of later `-m` resolution
**Pros:** Eliminates import-path drift entirely.
**Cons:** Pulls the design back toward explicit file execution and may complicate the installed-entrypoint story.
**Effort:** Medium

## Acceptance Criteria

- [ ] Bootstrap module execution for `verify`, `promote`, `migrate`, and `bootstrap-admin` uses the same isolation guarantees as module validation, or is otherwise bound to the exact validated module origin.
- [ ] A regression test proves contaminated `PYTHONPATH`/`PYTHONHOME`/cwd cannot alter `run_stack_bootstrap()` module execution after preflight approval.
- [ ] The fix preserves the intended canonical installed-entrypoint surface rather than falling back silently to compatibility wrappers.
close_reason: Fixed hardened module execution contract and added isolated bootstrap regression coverage

title: Resolve Review P2: Assert absence of raw payload content in redaction tests
description:
## Problem Statement

Current redaction tests assert only that the `source_record_redacted` flag is set. They do not verify that raw field values or malformed JSON text are actually absent from the emitted quarantine payload.

## Evidence

**Files:** `tests/test_ids_record_adapter.py`, `scripts/ids_record_adapter.py`
**Line(s):** redaction tests check the flag but not absence of original keys, values, or raw JSON text.

**Why this is a problem:**
A regression could leak full payload content while still satisfying the current assertions.

## Proposed Solutions

### Option A — [Recommended] Add absence-based redaction assertions
**Pros:** Locks the security behavior instead of checking only a marker flag.
**Cons:** Slightly more explicit fixture assertions.
**Effort:** Small

### Option B — Keep flag-only checks
**Pros:** Smaller tests.
**Cons:** Does not prove actual redaction.
**Effort:** Small

## Acceptance Criteria

- [ ] Default redaction tests assert that representative source keys, values, and raw malformed JSON text are absent.
- [ ] Opt-in raw capture tests remain as positive controls.
- [ ] A regression that emits full raw content fails the suite.
close_reason: Completed: redaction tests now assert raw payload content is absent by default

title: Resolve Review P2: Preserve runtime batching boundary across windows
description:
## Problem Statement

The daemon calls `runtime_runner.finalize()` after each processed capture window, which collapses the downstream batching boundary into a per-window flush. That makes the planned `capture window + extractor runtime + micro-batch flush interval` latency model largely moot and increases runtime churn.

## Evidence

**File:** `scripts/ids_live_sensor.py`
**Line(s):** window processing path around runtime finalize calls

- Reviewer evidence from architecture highlighted the per-window finalize behavior around the runtime handoff in `scripts/ids_live_sensor.py`.

**Why this is a problem:**
The planned architecture keeps the runtime stage window-agnostic and flush-controlled. Forcing finalize after each window weakens that separation and changes the operational latency/batching contract.

## Proposed Solutions

### Option A — [Recommended] Decouple window processing from runtime finalization
**Pros:** Preserves the planned batching boundary and makes flush controls meaningful.
**Cons:** Requires clearer lifecycle ownership between daemon and runtime runner.
**Effort:** Medium

### Option B — Re-document the runtime as explicitly per-window
**Pros:** Smaller code change.
**Cons:** Rewrites the intended architecture and likely hurts throughput under higher traffic.
**Effort:** Small

## Acceptance Criteria

- [ ] Window ingestion does not force a full runtime finalize after every closed window.
- [ ] Runtime flush/finalize remains an independent control surface.
- [ ] Tests cover successive windows without per-window forced finalize semantics.
close_reason: Completed: daemon preserves runtime batching boundary and flush/finalize are now independent of each capture window

title: Add Regression Coverage And Docs For UI Redesign
description:
## Goal
Close the redesign with regression coverage, compatibility verification, and doc updates.

## What
- Update route/rendering tests for the new IA, shell, and compatibility redirects.
- Update reporting assertions if reports gain new summary/trend treatment.
- Update operator documentation anywhere route/product naming changes matter.
- Run the relevant operator-console pytest suites and ensure the final surface stays wired through the canonical server/app path.

## Why
This feature has HIGH-risk route and shell changes; review will look for EXISTS / SUBSTANTIVE / WIRED and direct route references already exist in tests and docs.

## File Scope
- tests/test_ids_operator_console_web.py
- tests/test_ids_operator_console_config.py
- tests/test_ids_operator_console_reporting.py
- docs/ids_operator_console_operations.md

## Verification Criteria

- Legacy redirect behavior is covered.
- Runtime/app-factory wiring and operator docs match the shipped UI vocabulary.

## Context from Planning
From approach.md: validating must inspect redirect strategy and route-wiring coherence.

## Institutional Learnings
- From history/learnings/20260328-operator-console-runtime-wiring.md: verify the wired runtime, not just the existence of templates/routes.
- From history/learnings/critical-patterns.md: canonical contracts must execute what they claim.
acceptance_criteria:
Verification Commands

- rg -n /overview|/operations|/dashboard|/anomalies tests/test_ids_operator_console_web.py tests/test_ids_operator_console_config.py docs/ids_operator_console_operations.md

title: Resolve Review P3: Replace wildcard compatibility wrapper re-exports
description:
## Problem Statement

Several compatibility wrappers still re-export their canonical modules via wildcard imports. That keeps the wrapper layer opaque and makes the phase-1 compatibility surface harder to audit and reason about.

Source epic traceability: `ids_ml_new-br4g` fresh reviewing rerun.

## Evidence

**Files:** `scripts/ids_operator_console/web.py`, `scripts/ids_operator_console_preflight.py`, `scripts/ids_live_sensor_preflight.py`

## Acceptance Criteria

- [ ] Replace wildcard wrapper imports with explicit re-exports or explicit delegation.
- [ ] Preserve the same CLI/help/runtime behavior for the affected wrappers.
- [ ] Wrapper smoke/tests continue to pass.

> KNOWN PATTERN: Matches `20260331-repo-structure-wrapper-contracts.md`
> Previous occurrence: `history/learnings/20260331-repo-structure-wrapper-contracts.md`

title: Resolve Review P2: Deduplicate shadowed live sensor health tests
description:
## Problem Statement

One relocated runtime-health test function is defined twice under the same Python name, so the later definition silently overwrites the earlier one. The suite therefore misses one of the intended malformed-summary scenarios even though the file appears comprehensive at a glance.

## Evidence

Files:
- `tests/runtime/test_ids_live_sensor_health.py`

Why this is a problem:
- Python keeps only the last function definition with a given name.
- This creates an invisible coverage hole in a refactor whose primary safety net is preserved behavior under the moved runtime package.

## Proposed Solutions

### Option A - Recommended: rename/split the duplicated test cases and keep both scenarios
Pros: restores the missing coverage with minimal risk.
Cons: requires a small cleanup in one test module.
Effort: Small

### Option B - delete one case intentionally and document the dropped scenario
Pros: simpler if the scenario is redundant.
Cons: loses explicit edge-case coverage.
Effort: Small

## Acceptance Criteria

- [ ] Remove the duplicate test-name collision in `tests/runtime/test_ids_live_sensor_health.py`.
- [ ] Preserve coverage for both malformed-summary scenarios intended by the current file.
- [ ] The updated test module still passes under `pytest`.
close_reason: Completed: deduplicated shadowed malformed-summary tests in runtime health coverage

title: Resolve Review P3: Decouple core adapter imports from repo bootstrap
description:
## Problem Statement

The core adapter module mutates `sys.path` and builds filesystem-backed defaults at import time. This does not break the current repo use case, but it couples the reusable adapter API to the checkout layout and makes embedding or isolated imports harder than necessary.

## Evidence

**File:** `scripts/ids_record_adapter.py`
**Line(s):** 11-18, 96-105

The module bootstraps repo imports and constructs default contract objects as import-time side effects.

## Proposed Solutions

### Option A — [Recommended] Move bootstrap concerns to the CLI entrypoint and lazy factories
**Pros:** Keeps the core API side-effect free and easier to embed.
**Cons:** Some refactoring of module initialization.
**Effort:** Medium

### Option B — Keep the current import-time bootstrap
**Pros:** Simple for this repo.
**Cons:** Preserves tight coupling to the local checkout structure.
**Effort:** Small

## Acceptance Criteria

- [ ] Importing the reusable adapter module does not mutate `sys.path` or require repo-layout side effects.
- [ ] CLI behavior remains unchanged after bootstrap concerns move to explicit factories or the entrypoint.
close_reason: Completed: moved repo bootstrap concerns out of module import side effects

title: Fix 4 failing tests (M1)
close_reason: M1 feature complete. All Phase 1 beads closed. 590/590 pytest pass. Commit 39777b5.

title: Phase 1 / Story 1: Add editable install fixture + fix service unit assertion
description:
# Phase 1 / Story 1: Add editable install fixture + fix service unit assertion

## Summary

Fix the 4 failing tests in the repo test suite by adding a session-scoped pytest fixture that ensures `ids-ml-new` is installed as editable into the current interpreter's site-packages, and by updating one stale assertion in the live-sensor service-unit test. This closes audit finding M1 without touching any production code, service units, or security contracts.

## Phase Context

**Phase:** Phase 1 — Fix test drift
**What Changes:** `python -m pytest tests/ -q` reports `590 passed, 0 failed` on both local dev machines (without pre-installing the package) and CI. No production behavior changes.
**Unlocks Next:** M2 (CI/CD pipeline) can be safely added with confidence that all tests are green.

## Story Context

**Story:** Story 1 — Fix test drift (editable install fixture + assertion update)
**What Happens:** This single bead delivers the entire phase: session fixture in `tests/conftest.py`, one-line assertion fix in `tests/runtime/test_ids_live_sensor.py`, and verification that 590/590 tests pass.
**Contributes To:** Entire Phase 1 exit state (this is the only story in the phase).
**Unlocks:** Feature completion + M2 work.

## Planning Context

From `history/fix-failing-tests-m1/approach.md`:

- Fix A (fixture) is chosen over alternatives Skip/Remove `-I`/Add cwd/Create separate venv because it is the only approach that preserves the security contract of `ids/ops/module_validation.py::_run_module_check` while making tests pass in any environment.
- Fix B (assertion update) is preferred over "revert service unit to script path" because the service unit is the canonical form for installable packages — reverting would break packaging.
- Both fixes ship in one commit because Phase 1 exit state requires all tests green simultaneously.

## Institutional Learnings (MANDATORY — DO NOT VIOLATE)

### [20260403] Bind Privileged Bootstrap Execution To The Validated Interpreter Contract

From `history/learnings/critical-patterns.md`:
> "Preflight approval is meaningless if privileged bootstrap can still resolve code through a different interpreter, `cwd`, or inherited `PYTHON*` state. Future module-based bootstrap paths must run under the exact validated interpreter/env contract."

**Application to this bead:**
- DO NOT modify `ids/ops/module_validation.py` to add `cwd=REPO_ROOT`, remove `-I`, or relax env scrubbing.
- DO NOT pass `PYTHONPATH` into the subprocess.
- The fixture MUST use `sys.executable` (the same interpreter pytest is running under), not spawn a separate venv.

### [20260403] Prove Editable Installs In A Scrubbed Environment

> "In-tree or already-warmed verification is not enough to prove an install contract because it can hide missing source files, templates, or package data. Future packaging work should always run a fresh editable-install proof."

**Application to this bead:**
- The fixture MUST verify the install worked by calling `importlib.metadata.distribution("ids-ml-new")` after `pip install -e .`, not trust the pip exit code alone.
- If verification fails post-install, the fixture MUST raise `RuntimeError` with stdout+stderr captured.

### [20260330] Pin Multi-Token Runtime Contracts With End-To-End Tokenization Tests

> "Real risk lived in the tokenization chain across systemd, shell expansion, and argparse. The issue only became truly closed after tests exercised both the success round-trip and the missing-argument failure path."

**Application to this bead:**
- In `tests/runtime/test_ids_live_sensor.py`, when fixing line 513, KEEP ALL OTHER ASSERTIONS in `test_service_unit_keeps_preflight_and_stdout_journal_contract` intact. That test has 14 other tokenization assertions (shlex.split of ExecStart, the `--dumpcap-binary`, `--extractor-command-prefix` flags, the `StandardOutput=journal` line, etc.). Those are critical contracts.

### [20260331] Treat Compatibility Wrappers As Executable Contracts

> "Wrapper stability is not real unless CI exercises those wrappers directly."

**Application to this bead:**
- The fixture ensures tests exercise the same install contract that CI will use. No path hacks, no fallback behavior — if the package is not installable, tests MUST fail loudly.

## File Scope

### Files to modify (exactly 2)

**1. `tests/conftest.py`** (add ~35 lines)

Add this fixture at the top of the file (after existing imports, before any other fixtures):

```python
import importlib.metadata
import subprocess
import sys
from pathlib import Path

import pytest

_REPO_ROOT = Path(__file__).resolve().parents[1]


@pytest.fixture(scope="session", autouse=True)
def _ensure_editable_install() -> None:
    """Ensure ids-ml-new is installed as editable before any test runs.

    Rationale: tests/ops/test_ids_operator_console_ops.py preflight tests
    invoke ids.ops.module_validation._run_module_check which spawns a
    subprocess with `python -I` (isolated mode), cwd=python_binary.parent,
    and a PYTHON*-scrubbed environment. In that subprocess, ids.console.server
    can only be imported if the ids-ml-new package is present in the
    interpreter's site-packages. This mirrors the production contract
    documented in critical-pattern [20260403] "Bind Privileged Bootstrap
    Execution To The Validated Interpreter Contract".

    This fixture is idempotent: if the package is already installed, it
    is a no-op. Otherwise, it runs `pip install -e .` once per session
    and verifies success via importlib.metadata.distribution.
    """
    try:
        importlib.metadata.distribution("ids-ml-new")
        return
    except importlib.metadata.PackageNotFoundError:
        pass

    result = subprocess.run(
        [sys.executable, "-m", "pip", "install", "-e", ".", "--quiet"],
        cwd=_REPO_ROOT,
        capture_output=True,
        text=True,
    )
    if result.returncode != 0:
        raise RuntimeError(
            "Failed to install ids-ml-new as editable package.\n"
            f"stdout: {result.stdout}\n"
            f"stderr: {result.stderr}"
        )

    try:
        importlib.metadata.distribution("ids-ml-new")
    except importlib.metadata.PackageNotFoundError as exc:
        raise RuntimeError(
            "pip install -e . reported success but ids-ml-new still not importable"
        ) from exc
```

If `tests/conftest.py` already has imports matching these, merge them; do not duplicate.

**2. `tests/runtime/test_ids_live_sensor.py`** (change 1 line at line 513)

```python
# BEFORE:
assert "ids_live_sensor_preflight.py" in content

# AFTER:
assert "-m ids.ops.live_sensor_preflight" in content
```

ALL OTHER LINES IN THE FILE MUST REMAIN UNCHANGED. In particular, lines 510-512 and 514-537 are part of the tokenization contract and must not be touched.

### Files that MUST NOT be modified

- `ids/ops/module_validation.py` — security contract; verify with `git diff ids/ops/module_validation.py` returns empty after work
- `ids/ops/operator_console_preflight.py` — callsite of the security contract
- `deploy/systemd/ids-live-sensor.service` — service unit is already correct
- `tests/ops/test_ids_operator_console_ops.py` — the 3 failing tests don't need logic changes; fixture makes them pass
- `pyproject.toml` — no dependency changes
- `requirements.txt` — no dependency changes
- Any production source file in `ids/` or `ml_pipeline/`

## Dependencies

None. This is the first and only bead in Phase 1.

## Verification Criteria

Run ALL of these checks after the edit. ALL must pass:

### Hard checks (blocking)

1. `python -m pytest tests/ -q --tb=line` completes with exit code 0 and output shows `590 passed`
2. `git diff ids/` produces empty output
3. `git diff deploy/` produces empty output
4. `git diff pyproject.toml requirements.txt` produces empty output
5. `git diff --stat tests/` shows exactly two files changed: `tests/conftest.py` and `tests/runtime/test_ids_live_sensor.py`
6. `python -m pip show ids-ml-new` returns package info (Name: ids-ml-new)
7. Running `python -m pytest tests/ -q` a second time in the same venv still shows `590 passed` (idempotency check)

### Soft checks (quality)

8. The fixture has a clear docstring referencing critical-pattern [20260403]
9. No `print()` or `logging` statements leaked into the fixture
10. Fixture does not create any new files in the repo (no `.pip-install-cache` or similar side effects)
11. Commit message is descriptive, e.g. `test(m1): ensure editable install fixture + fix service unit assertion (M1)`

## Pivot Signals

If any of these occur during execution, STOP and report — do not try to fix-forward:

- After adding fixture, the 3 preflight tests still fail with a different error (not "not importable") → root cause analysis needed, invoke debugging skill
- After fixing assertion, the service unit test still fails or another assertion in the same test starts failing → test drift is broader than expected
- Adding the fixture causes ≥1 regression in the 586 currently-passing tests → fixture scope is wrong, need to revisit
- `pip install -e .` fails on the dev machine due to broken Python environment → out of scope for M1, pause
- CI lacks permission to write to site-packages → may need to require CI to pre-install package before pytest

## Done Definition

Bead is closed only when ALL 7 hard checks pass AND the commit is made with the bead ID in the commit message.

## Estimated Effort

15-30 minutes for a single worker (read CONTEXT.md + approach.md, apply 2 edits, run pytest, verify diff, commit).
close_reason: M1 test drift fixed: 590/590 pass (was 586/590). Commit 39777b5. Fixture uses isolated-subprocess check matching _run_module_check contract [20260403]. All forbidden paths (ids/, deploy/, pyproject.toml, requirements.txt) untouched. 14 tokenization assertions [20260330] in test_service_unit_keeps_preflight_and_stdout_journal_contract preserved.

title: Resolve Review P2: Add failure-path coverage for restore inventory and post-restore gating
description:
## Problem Statement

Restore verification is supposed to be a first-class stack contract under D13-D15, but the current tests only exercise one happy-path inventory case and two post-restore success cases. The implementation has substantial branch logic for selecting the effective backup directory, validating rebound secret references, classifying missing backup artifacts, and computing `post_restore_ready` from seven separate gates. Those failure paths are mostly untested, so regressions in the same-host restore contract can slip through while the suite still reports green.

## Evidence

**File:** `scripts/ids_same_host_stack.py`
**Line(s):** 501-675, 756-849

```python
def _require_operator_backup_dir(...):
    ...
    backup_dirs = sorted(..., reverse=True)
    if not backup_dirs:
        raise FileNotFoundError(...)

def _evaluate_secret_reference(...):
    if source == "file":
        ok = current_source is not None and Path(current_source).is_file()
    ...
    if ok and reject_placeholders:
        ok = normalized not in PLACEHOLDER_SECRET_VALUES

post_restore_ready = all([
    inventory_payload["inventory_ready"],
    live_sensor_revalidation["ok"],
    recovery_payload["recovery_ready"],
    notification_ok,
    final_status.get("ready"),
    final_smoke.get("ready"),
    bundle_visibility["ok"],
])
```

**File:** `tests/test_ids_same_host_stack_manage.py`
**Line(s):** 654-696, 699-854

```python
def test_build_stack_restore_inventory_restore_or_post_restore_checks_minimum_inventory(...)
def test_run_stack_post_restore_check_restore_or_post_restore_redrives_notifications(...)
def test_run_stack_post_restore_check_restore_or_post_restore_skips_disabled_notification(...)
```

**Why this is a problem:**
The feature's restore story is explicitly about failing closed on missing host state and proving recovery criteria after restore; without targeted failure-path tests, the highest-risk verification surface is only checked in the success direction.

## Proposed Solutions

### Option A - [Recommended] Add restore failure-matrix tests
**Pros:** Pins down the recovery contract where the approach identified the highest integration risk.
**Cons:** Requires a few more fixture variants.
**Effort:** Medium

### Option B - Rely on component tests only
**Pros:** Less new test code.
**Cons:** Misses the stack-specific wiring and gating logic this feature introduced.
**Effort:** Small

## Acceptance Criteria

- [ ] Add restore-inventory tests for missing manifest / backup DB, backup-root inputs that must select the newest `backup-*` directory, and secret-reference failures such as missing file rebound or placeholder inline values.
- [ ] Add post-restore tests that prove `post_restore_ready` becomes false when any required gate fails, especially final `status`, final `smoke`, or bundle visibility re-establishment.
- [ ] Add a notification-enabled failure test showing that a still-degraded `notify-status` / `notify-redrive` result blocks `post_restore_ready`.
close_reason: Resolved in follow-up review-fix swarm: bootstrap verification, degraded payloads, redaction, and coverage expanded

title: Resolve Review P2: Enforce closed-set label boundaries in derived family views
description:
## Plain-Language Summary

The derived family views claim to create a closed, trustworthy label space for both the attack-only lane and the direct multiclass comparison lane, but the builder currently forwards rows more loosely than the contract says. That means later training and comparison can silently depend on upstream artifact cleanliness instead of the view builder enforcing the boundary itself.

## What The Code Does Today

- `attack_only_filter` removes only `Benign`, so any non-benign family row can still pass through the attack-only view.
- `direct_multiclass_filter` returns the input frame unchanged for `train` / `val` / `test`, so unexpected attack families are not explicitly excluded from the declared closed set.

## Why This Is A Problem

- Decision D6 in practice: the first supervised family space is only `DDoS`, `DoS`, `Mirai`, `Spoofing`, and `Web-Based`.
- Decision D7 in practice: `BruteForce` and `Recon` stay out of supervised training and act as OOD probes.
- If the builder does not enforce those rules, the Phase 1 comparison can drift silently and downstream models can train on labels that were supposed to be held out.

## Concrete Failure Scenario

- A refreshed binary artifact accidentally includes one `Recon` row in `train.parquet`.
- The direct multiclass view keeps that row because the non-holdout path returns `frame.copy()`.
- The baseline now learns on an OOD family it was supposed to hold out, and Story 3 compares the wrong experiment without any obvious failure.

## Evidence

**File:** `ml_pipeline/data_prep/prepare_iot_diad_family_views.py`

The view filters currently document a closed-set contract but do not fully enforce it for non-holdout splits.

## Proposed Solutions

### Option A - Recommended: Fail closed on declared label sets
**Pros:** Keeps the view artifact authoritative and reproducible.
**Cons:** Requires explicit validation logic and a few extra tests.
**Effort:** Small

### Option B - Trust the source artifact upstream
**Pros:** Less code in the view builder.
**Cons:** Leaves the phase boundary soft and makes regressions easier to miss.
**Effort:** Small

## Acceptance Criteria

- [ ] Attack-only derivation only keeps declared in-distribution attack families on train/val/test and OOD probe families on the OOD split.
- [ ] Direct multiclass derivation only keeps `Benign` plus the declared known families on train/val/test.
- [ ] The builder fails clearly when unexpected labels appear instead of silently passing them through.

title: Resolve Review P1: Stdin pipeline buffers until EOF instead of emitting realtime batches
description:
P1 review candidate 2
Title: Resolve Review P1: Stdin pipeline buffers until EOF instead of emitting realtime batches
Severity rationale: blocking behavioral defect against locked decision D3
Evidence:
- `read_jsonl_stream()` materializes the entire stream into a list before processing at [scripts/ids_realtime_pipeline.py](F:/Work/IDS_ML_New/scripts/ids_realtime_pipeline.py):151-156.
- `run_pipeline_stream()` iterates over that preloaded list at [scripts/ids_realtime_pipeline.py](F:/Work/IDS_ML_New/scripts/ids_realtime_pipeline.py):202, so a long-lived stdin pipe will not validate, flush, or emit alerts until the producer closes the stream.
- This violates D3 in [history/ids-pre-model-realtime-pipeline/CONTEXT.md](F:/Work/IDS_ML_New/history/ids-pre-model-realtime-pipeline/CONTEXT.md):30-35, which requires near-realtime micro-batch processing and immediate alert emission per short batch.
Impact:
- The new runtime behaves like end-of-file batch processing for stdin instead of an online IDS boundary.
Acceptance criteria:
- Stream processing consumes stdin incrementally line by line without preloading the full stream.
- Valid records can flush on batch size or flush interval before EOF.
- Tests cover a non-EOF stream progression scenario, not only finite StringIO drained at the end.
close_reason: Fixed incremental stdin processing and pre-EOF micro-batch flushing in ids_realtime_pipeline.py; covered by regression tests.

title: Spike: Can production-facing runtime and bundle commands share one Linux path-default contract without reopening activation or path-safety seams?

Validate the path-normalization strategy before execution.

Question:

What to prove:
- The in-scope production-facing commands can consume one shared default/config contract.
- Historical non-production ML workflow defaults can remain out of the production boundary if necessary.
- Activation-record selection and fail-closed/path-containment behavior remain unchanged.

NO condition:

title: Reprove canonical packaged contracts after review follow-ups
description: Add a final integrated proof pass for the review-followup wave. This bead is a proof-and-assertion bead: it may update assertions inside the existing listed proof suites, but it must not introduce a new runtime or deployment path and should not create new proof files unless the listed suites cannot express the required assertion. The proof must inline and revalidate these contracts after all upstream follow-up beads land: D4 means a fresh Linux-host-style repo install can invoke the canonical packaged entrypoints and complete the documented proof surface through preflight, status, and smoke. D6 means installed entrypoints resolve directly to canonical ids/* modules, while scripts/* remain compatibility-only surfaces rather than the primary packaged path. D7 means machine-specific paths and host-local values stay explicit through config or inputs, with no required workstation path assumptions. D8 means production bundle activation remains on the explicit verify -> promote -> active_bundle record flow, with no raw bundle-path production fallback. This bead owns the end-of-wave proof that those four contracts still hold together in the post-review state.
acceptance_criteria: The bead extends the existing listed suites instead of creating new proof surfaces unless strictly necessary. The final focused proof explicitly re-validates: fresh-host-style packaged execution for preflight/status/smoke; installed entrypoint mapping to ids/* modules; explicit shared path-default behavior for affected callers; and activation-record-driven bundle selection after verify/promote. Verify: python -m pytest tests/ops/test_ids_repo_installable_bootstrap_proof.py tests/ops/test_ids_repo_installable_surface.py tests/ops/test_ids_same_host_stack_manage.py tests/ops/test_ids_operator_console_preflight.py tests/runtime/test_ids_realtime_pipeline.py tests/core/test_ids_path_defaults.py tests/runtime/test_ids_inference.py tests/runtime/test_ids_record_adapter.py tests/ops/test_ids_model_bundle_manage.py -q exits 0.
close_reason: Completed: integrated proof suite passes on the fresh isolated install surface

title: Make shared repo-root path resolution fully explicit
description: Make the shared repo-root resolution contract explicit in ids/core/path_defaults.py and in the production-adjacent shared callers that depend on it. The contract to implement is: resolve_repo_root() uses IDS_REPO_ROOT when it is set; otherwise it falls back to the current checkout root derived from the file location; it must not silently switch to /opt/ids_ml_new just because that path happens to exist on the host. If a production caller needs /opt/ids_ml_new, that path must come from explicit host config, repo_root input, or IDS_REPO_ROOT rather than implicit filesystem-shape detection. Scope includes the helper itself plus any directly affected callers or tests such as ids/runtime/inference.py, ids/runtime/adapter/record_adapter.py, ml_pipeline/packaging/package_final_model.py, and tests/core/test_ids_path_defaults.py as needed to keep D7 explicit.
acceptance_criteria: The final repo-root fallback order is explicit: IDS_REPO_ROOT override first, then checkout-root fallback, with no silent /opt/ids_ml_new preference. Shared callers that depend on resolve_repo_root() either continue to use that explicit contract or pass their own repo_root or host-config value intentionally. D7 remains satisfied: no workstation path and no implicit host-shape path becomes a required runtime assumption. Verify: python -m pytest tests/core/test_ids_path_defaults.py tests/runtime/test_ids_inference.py tests/runtime/test_ids_record_adapter.py tests/ops/test_ids_model_bundle_manage.py -q exits 0.

title: Resolve Review P2: Reduce notification detail exposure on unauthenticated readiness surfaces
description:
## Problem Statement

The readiness payload now exposes notification internals to unauthenticated callers, including delivery target and recent error detail.

## Evidence

**Files:**
- `scripts/ids_operator_console/health.py`
- `tests/test_ids_operator_console_web.py`

**Why this is a problem:**
`/readyz` is commonly reachable by load balancers and health probes. Surfacing `target`, backlog counts, and raw-ish `last_error` text to unauthenticated callers leaks operational metadata and creates an avoidable reconnaissance surface.

## Proposed Solutions

### Option A — Recommended: keep `/readyz` actionable but sanitize it
**Pros:** Preserves operator visibility while removing sensitive delivery target/error detail from public health responses.
**Cons:** May require splitting internal operator detail from external readiness detail.
**Effort:** Small

### Option B — require auth for all notification detail surfaces
**Pros:** Strongest confidentiality boundary.
**Cons:** Changes current health-probe ergonomics and may complicate load balancer integration.
**Effort:** Medium

## Acceptance Criteria

- [ ] Unauthenticated `/readyz` no longer exposes Telegram chat target or overly specific error text.
- [ ] Operators still have an explicit authenticated or CLI surface for detailed notification diagnosis.
- [ ] Tests cover the sanitized readiness contract.
close_reason: Review fix pass verified: /readyz now sanitizes notification target and error detail while notify-status keeps operator detail

title: Resolve Review P2: Prove secondary profile runtime handoff end to end
description:
## Problem Statement

The current end-to-end runtime compatibility proof exists only for the primary profile. The secondary profile is covered for mapping and streaming, but not for adapter-to-runtime handoff through the existing realtime pipeline.

## Evidence

**Files:** `tests/test_ids_record_adapter.py`, `history/ids-structured-record-adapter/approach.md`
**Line(s):** runtime handoff coverage exercises only `cicflowmeter_primary_v1`.

**Why this is a problem:**
The plan marked downstream compatibility as HIGH risk, and the feature’s extensibility proof requires both shipped profiles to be runtime-consumable.

## Proposed Solutions

### Option A — [Recommended] Add secondary-profile runtime handoff coverage
**Pros:** Closes the remaining high-risk verification gap.
**Cons:** Adds one more integration-style test.
**Effort:** Small

### Option B — Rely on field-level mapping tests only
**Pros:** Smaller suite.
**Cons:** Leaves one supported profile only partially verified end to end.
**Effort:** Small

## Acceptance Criteria

- [ ] A valid secondary-profile record is adapted and accepted by `ids_realtime_pipeline.py` without extra translation.
- [ ] Tests assert zero runtime quarantines on the valid secondary handoff path.
- [ ] The adapted record preserves normalized passthrough metadata including `adapter_profile`.

title: Phase 1 / Story 1.3: Rewrite web.py with all routes + login template (TDD — tests first)
description:
## Phase Context
Phase: Phase 1 — Shell, tokens, and auth
What Changes: New web.py is the complete app factory. All 12 HTML routes + JSON APIs registered. Login/logout work.
Unlocks Next: Phase 2 workers implement page templates against stable route contracts.

## Story Context
Story: Story 1.3 — New web.py skeleton + login/logout (TDD)
TDD ORDER: 1) Write failing tests. 2) Write web.py. 3) Write login.html. 4) Tests pass.

## Task

### A: Write failing tests FIRST
tests/console/test_ids_operator_console_auth.py:
- valid login -> session set -> redirect /overview
- invalid login -> 200 error, no session
- logout with CSRF -> session cleared -> redirect /login
- unauthenticated GET /overview -> 303 to /login

tests/console/test_ids_operator_console_web.py:
- GET /dashboard -> 303 to /overview (legacy redirect)
- GET /anomalies -> 303 to /operations (legacy redirect)
- GET /live-logs, /suppression-rules, /system-health all registered (return 200 or 501, not 404)

title: Extract feature and bundle contracts into ids.core
description:
Extract the minimal cross-domain contract layer into `ids/core`.

What:
- Move only true shared contracts into `ids/core`, starting with feature-schema and model-bundle activation surfaces.
- Keep `ids/core` narrow; do not turn it into a generic dumping ground.
- Update canonical imports so later runtime/ops/console beads consume the new core boundary instead of flat script modules.

Context from Planning:
- approach.md classifies `ids/core` extraction as HIGH risk because these contracts are imported broadly.
- D9 only allows real contracts, schemas, config primitives, and narrow utilities in shared/core.

Institutional Learnings:
- Keep production model selection on one activation contract.
- Keep host-service preflight/deploy seams explicit rather than hiding them in generic helpers.

File scope:
- ids/core/**
- scripts/ids_feature_contract.py
- scripts/ids_model_bundle.py
- imports in directly dependent runtime/ops modules
- matching tests for core contract movement

Acceptance:
- Canonical feature-contract and model-bundle modules live under `ids/core`.
- Imports flow through the new canonical core package without reopening split bundle semantics.
- `ids/core` stays intentionally narrow.

Verify:
- `python -m pytest tests/test_ids_feature_contract.py tests/test_ids_model_bundle.py tests/test_ids_inference.py -q`

Validated constraints:
- ids.core is a leaf-only contract slice: pure dataclasses, validators, and manifest builders only.
- Keep repo-path defaults, activation resolution, promotion, rollback, and status helpers out of core.
- Preserve one-way imports: runtime and ops may import core; core may not import them.
- Keep the single activation contract and feature-schema equality invariant intact.
close_reason: Completed: extracted core feature contract and routed bundle metadata imports through ids.core

title: Resolve Review P1: Add wrapper smoke coverage for runtime entrypoints
description:
## Problem Statement

The refactor moved runtime implementation behind `ids.runtime`, but the resulting test suite mostly exercises canonical modules directly and does not smoke the compatibility wrappers that phase 1 promised to preserve. A broken `python -m scripts.ids_inference` / `scripts.ids_live_sensor*` entrypoint would bypass the current suite even though wrapper-backed CLI stability was part of the bead acceptance.

## Evidence

Files:
- `scripts/ids_inference.py`
- `scripts/ids_live_capture.py`
- `scripts/ids_live_sensor.py`
- `scripts/ids_live_sensor_health.py`
- `scripts/ids_live_sensor_sinks.py`
- `tests/runtime/test_ids_inference.py`
- `tests/runtime/test_ids_live_capture.py`
- `tests/runtime/test_ids_live_sensor.py`
- `tests/runtime/test_ids_live_sensor_health.py`
- `tests/runtime/test_ids_live_sensor_sinks.py`

Why this is a problem:
- Beads `ids_ml_new-br4g.4` and `ids_ml_new-br4g.11` required wrapper compatibility in phase 1.
- The migrated tests target `ids.runtime.*` directly, so wrapper regressions can ship unnoticed.

## Proposed Solutions

### Option A - Recommended: add focused wrapper smoke tests
Pros: directly validates the public compatibility surface with low maintenance cost.
Cons: adds a few more process-level tests.
Effort: Small

### Option B - rely on manual `--help` checks only
Pros: lower implementation cost.
Cons: does not protect against future regressions in CI and is easy to forget.
Effort: Small

## Acceptance Criteria

- [ ] Add smoke tests that invoke the runtime compatibility wrappers used in phase 1.
- [ ] Cover at least `scripts.ids_inference`, `scripts.ids_live_capture`, `scripts.ids_live_sensor`, `scripts.ids_live_sensor_health`, and `scripts.ids_live_sensor_sinks`.
- [ ] The smoke tests run in CI as part of the normal pytest suite.
close_reason: Completed

title: Resolve Review P1: Add smoke tests for ops and stack compatibility wrappers
description:
## Problem Statement

The deploy-facing ops wrappers remain part of the supported phase-1 surface, but the post-refactor test suite validates `ids.ops.*` modules directly instead of exercising the `scripts.*` entrypoints that runbooks and systemd automation call. This leaves a production orchestration path effectively unverified.

## Evidence

Files:
- `scripts/ids_live_sensor_preflight.py`
- `scripts/ids_same_host_stack.py`
- `scripts/ids_same_host_stack_manage.py`
- `tests/ops/test_ids_live_sensor_preflight.py`
- `tests/ops/test_ids_same_host_stack_manage.py`

Why this is a problem:
- Beads `ids_ml_new-br4g.6` and `ids_ml_new-br4g.13` required wrapper compatibility for current operational commands.
- The updated tests import `ids.ops.*` directly and do not prove that the wrapper layer still behaves correctly for deployment automation.

## Proposed Solutions

### Option A - Recommended: add wrapper-smoke tests for operational entrypoints
Pros: protects real deploy/runbook surfaces and catches regression quickly.
Cons: adds process-level tests around CLI wrappers.
Effort: Small

### Option B - rely on ad hoc manual smoke checks
Pros: lower upfront effort.
Cons: regressions will not be caught automatically.
Effort: Small

## Acceptance Criteria

- [ ] Add smoke coverage for `scripts.ids_live_sensor_preflight`, `scripts.ids_same_host_stack`, and `scripts.ids_same_host_stack_manage`.
- [ ] Verify wrapper invocation reaches the canonical `ids.ops` implementation without import/runtime errors.
- [ ] Include the new smoke checks in the regular pytest path or equivalent automated verification.
close_reason: Added wrapper smoke coverage for ops phase-1 compatibility paths

title: Resolve Review P1: Restore same-host stack manage direct-script contract
description:
## Problem Statement

The same-host stack manage compatibility surface is still documented and wired as a direct script entrypoint (`python scripts/ids_same_host_stack_manage.py ...`), but that path now fails before argparse runs. The wrapper imports `ids.ops.same_host_stack_manage` without adding the repo root to `sys.path`, and the canonical module's own direct-file bootstrap inserts the `ids/` directory instead of the repository root. This breaks the phase-1 compatibility contract and leaves deploy-facing docs pointing at a dead command.

## Evidence

**Files:** `scripts/ids_same_host_stack_manage.py`, `ids/ops/same_host_stack_manage.py`, `docs/current/operations/ids_same_host_stack_operations.md`

```python
# scripts/ids_same_host_stack_manage.py
from ids.ops.same_host_stack_manage import *

# ids/ops/same_host_stack_manage.py
if __package__ in (None, "):
    REPO_ROOT = Path(__file__).resolve().parents[1]
```

**Reproduction:**
- `python scripts/ids_same_host_stack_manage.py --help`
- Result: `ModuleNotFoundError: No module named 'ids'`

**Why this is a problem:**
The repository refactor explicitly preserves compatibility entrypoints in phase 1, and the current operations docs still instruct operators to use this direct script path. A documented deploy-facing command is broken right now.

## Proposed Solutions

### Option A - Recommended: restore direct-file bootstrap contract
**Pros:** Preserves documented operator workflow and honors phase-1 compatibility.
**Cons:** Requires touching both the thin wrapper and the canonical module bootstrap path.
**Effort:** Small

### Option B - Narrow the supported contract to `python -m ...`
**Pros:** Simpler import story.
**Cons:** Requires updating all current docs/runbooks and any operator automation that still uses direct file execution.
**Effort:** Medium

## Acceptance Criteria

- [ ] `python scripts/ids_same_host_stack_manage.py --help` exits successfully.
- [ ] Representative documented direct-file commands for the same-host stack path execute far enough to print argparse help or structured output instead of import failure.
- [ ] Tests cover the supported direct-file compatibility path so this regression cannot silently return.
close_reason: Completed: restore same-host stack direct-script compatibility

title: Resolve Review P3: Replace wildcard wrapper exports with explicit compatibility shims
description:
## Problem Statement

Several compatibility wrappers use wildcard re-exports (`from ... import *`) instead of explicit delegation. That makes the compatibility surface implicit, weakens static analysis, and makes the wrapper seams less intentional than the phase-1 plan called for.

## Evidence

Files:
- `scripts/ids_same_host_stack.py`
- `scripts/ids_operator_console_manage.py`
- `scripts/stage_kaggle_benchmark.py`

Why this is a problem:
- Decision `D8` keeps wrappers as thin compatibility seams.
- Wildcard exports obscure exactly what the wrapper promises to preserve.

## Proposed Solutions

### Option A - Recommended: replace wildcard exports with explicit thin wrapper shims
Pros: clearer compatibility contract and easier static analysis.
Cons: slightly more boilerplate in wrappers.
Effort: Small

### Option B - keep wildcard exports and rely on comments
Pros: least code churn.
Cons: leaves the wrapper surface implicit.
Effort: Small

## Acceptance Criteria

- [ ] Replace wildcard re-exports in the identified compatibility wrappers with explicit delegation or explicit re-export lists.
- [ ] Keep the wrapper surfaces behaviorally compatible.
- [ ] Confirm the affected entrypoints still pass their smoke checks.
close_reason: Completed: explicit compatibility shims for wrapper exports

title: Resolve Review P2: Make realtime inferencer schema seam explicit
description:
## Problem Statement

`ids.runtime.realtime_pipeline` now derives its feature-schema location by dynamically probing `inferencer.config.feature_columns_path`. That makes the pipeline depend on a hidden collaborator implementation detail instead of an explicit runtime contract, so future inferencer refactors or alternate inferencer implementations can remain valid for scoring but still break the pipeline boundary.

## Evidence

**Files:** `ids/runtime/realtime_pipeline.py`, `tests/runtime/test_ids_realtime_pipeline.py`

**Why this is a problem:**
The feature claims one canonical runtime contract centered on the active bundle and explicit resolved config, but schema resolution is now split across runtime layers and accessed via `getattr(...)` on a private object shape. The new tests also have to manufacture fake `.config.feature_columns_path` objects purely to satisfy that hidden seam.

## Proposed Solutions

### Option A — [Recommended] Make the inferencer/schema seam explicit
**Pros:** Reduces coupling and makes the runtime boundary typed, documented, and easier to test.
**Cons:** Requires touching builder interfaces and test doubles.
**Effort:** Medium

### Option B — keep the implicit seam and document it
**Pros:** Smaller immediate change.
**Cons:** Preserves fragile coupling and hidden API surface.
**Effort:** Small

## Acceptance Criteria

- [ ] `ids.runtime.realtime_pipeline` no longer discovers schema location through dynamic introspection of `inferencer.config`.
- [ ] The inferencer/config boundary exposes feature-schema location through an explicit typed/public interface or by passing resolved config separately.
- [ ] Regression tests can supply an alternate inferencer stub without needing a hidden `.config.feature_columns_path` implementation detail.
notes: Verify: python -m pytest tests/runtime/test_ids_realtime_pipeline.py tests/runtime/test_ids_inference.py -q
close_reason: Completed: make realtime inferencer schema resolution explicit
