title: Consolidate shadow finder sitecustomize bodies into shared helper
description:
## Problem Statement

Source: review of ids_ml_new-4of7 (P3, code-quality + architecture reviewers).

The _ShadowFinder class body (env-var gated, find_spec method, sys.meta_path.insert) is duplicated between:
1. tests/ops/repo_installable_proof_support.py write_shadow_sitecustomize() (lines 131-158)
2. tests/ops/test_ids_repo_installable_bootstrap_proof.py _write_sitecustomize() (lines 95-125)

The bootstrap proof version adds an IDS_TEST_BYPASS_INTERFACE block before the shadow finder. If the shadow finder protocol changes, both copies must be updated in sync.

## Fix

Extract the shadow finder body as a shared string constant or function that both callers compose. The bootstrap proof version would prepend the interface bypass block to the shared content.

## Acceptance Criteria

- [ ] Shadow finder logic exists in one place (repo_installable_proof_support.py).
- [ ] Bootstrap proof test composes the shared content with its extra interface bypass.
- [ ] Both test suites pass unchanged.

## Notes

No dependencies. Test-only change.
Verify: python -m pytest tests/ops/test_ids_repo_installable_bootstrap_proof.py tests/ops/test_ids_repo_installable_surface.py -q
close_reason: Completed

title: Optimize test venv sharing with unified cache keys
description:
## Problem Statement

Source: review of ids_ml_new-4of7 (P3, architecture reviewer).

Test files call shared_editable_repo_python() with different prefix strings:
- test_ids_operator_console_preflight.py: f"preflight-proof-{os.getpid()}"
- test_ids_same_host_stack_manage.py: f"same-host-stack-proof-{os.getpid()}"

Since the prefix differs, each test file gets a separate cached venv even when running in the same pytest session (same PID). The expensive pip install -e runs at least twice per session. The PID adds no useful isolation (the cache identity already includes sys.executable and REPO_ROOT).

## Fix

Use a single shared cache key (e.g., "repo-installable-proof") across all test files. The stamp-based signature check handles staleness. If test isolation is needed for shadow .pth cleanup, handle that at the test level.

## Acceptance Criteria

- [ ] All test files that call shared_editable_repo_python use the same cache key.
- [ ] pip install -e runs once per session, not once per test file.
- [ ] Shadow .pth cleanup still works correctly across all tests.

## Notes

No dependencies. Performance improvement for test suite (saves ~5-7 min per duplicate install).
Verify: python -m pytest tests/ops/test_ids_operator_console_preflight.py tests/ops/test_ids_same_host_stack_manage.py tests/ops/test_ids_repo_installable_bootstrap_proof.py tests/ops/test_ids_repo_installable_surface.py -q
close_reason: Verified unified cache key and shared helper adoption

title: Add test coverage for probe-fail-then-repair path in shared_editable_repo_python
description:
## Problem Statement

Source: review of ids_ml_new-4of7 (P3, test-coverage reviewer).

shared_editable_repo_python() in repo_installable_proof_support.py has a probe-fail-repair codepath (lines 103-114): if _probe_editable_install() fails, it deletes the stamp, reinstalls, and reprobes. The cache tests in test_ids_repo_installable_surface.py mock run_command to always return success, so this path is never exercised. The ogbn bead acceptance criteria included "stale cache repair."

## Fix

Add a test where fake_run_command returns failure on the first probe call (matching -I -c "import ..."), then success on subsequent calls. Verify the reinstall happens and the stamp is rewritten.

## Acceptance Criteria

- [ ] A test exercises the probe-fail-repair path in shared_editable_repo_python.
- [ ] The test verifies that when the probe fails, a reinstall occurs and a new stamp is written.
- [ ] The test verifies that if the repair also fails, an AssertionError is raised.

## Notes

No dependencies. Test-only addition.
Verify: python -m pytest tests/ops/test_ids_repo_installable_surface.py -q
close_reason: Completed

title: Remove dead require_importable_module export from module_validation
description:
## Problem Statement

Source: review of ids_ml_new-4of7 (P3, test-coverage reviewer).

require_importable_module() at ids/ops/module_validation.py lines 132-147 has no production callers after this refactor. operator_console_preflight.py now uses _require_importable_module_from_trusted_root (which calls resolve_importable_module directly), and same_host_stack.py calls resolve_importable_module directly. The public require_importable_module is dead code with no tests. It is misleading API surface — a new caller might use it without trusted_root, silently bypassing the security boundary.

## Fix

Remove require_importable_module() from module_validation.py. Verify no imports reference it.

## Acceptance Criteria

- [ ] require_importable_module is removed from module_validation.py.
- [ ] No file imports or calls require_importable_module.
- [ ] All tests pass.

## Notes

Depends on: ids_ml_new-fuf5.1 (verify its still unused after the atomic subprocess merge which may change the API).
Verify: grep -r require_importable_module ids/ tests/ && python -m pytest tests/ops/ -q
close_reason: Completed: require_importable_module() removed after verifying no callers.

title: Fail module checks when repo_root directory is invalid
description:
## Problem Statement

Source: review of ids_ml_new-4of7 (P2, security reviewer).

In `validate_stack_preflight()` inside `ids/ops/same_host_stack.py`, when the `repo_root` directory check fails (directory does not exist, is not readable, etc.), `check_directory()` returns `None` and records the failure in `host_layout_checks`. However, subsequent module checks still proceed with `trusted_root=None`, which causes `resolve_importable_module()` to skip the trusted-root containment check entirely (line 120: `if trusted_root is not None:`).

This means individual module checks can report `ok: True` and `state: ready` even though the trust boundary cannot be verified. A downstream consumer reading the preflight JSON could see `host_layout_checks.model_manage_module.ok == True` without realizing the trusted-root enforcement was silently bypassed.

## Evidence

**File:** `ids/ops/same_host_stack.py`
**Lines:** 1069-1096

```python
repo_root = check_directory(config.repo_root, name="repo_root")
# ... repo_root can be None here ...
def check_module(module_name, *, name, python_binary_path):
    # ...
    checked, _origin = _resolve_importable_module(
        python_binary_path, module_name, name=name,
        trusted_root=Path(repo_root).resolve() if repo_root is not None else None,
        # ^^^ When repo_root is None, trusted_root is None, containment is skipped
    )
```

**File:** `ids/ops/module_validation.py`
**Line:** 120

```python
if trusted_root is not None:  # skipped when None
    trusted_root_path = Path(trusted_root).resolve()
    origin_path.relative_to(trusted_root_path)
```

## Concrete Failure Scenario

1. An operator runs stack preflight on a host where the repo_root directory has been temporarily unmounted or its permissions changed.
2. `check_directory(config.repo_root)` returns `None` and records `repo_root: {ok: False}`.
3. Module checks run without trusted-root enforcement.
4. A shadow module from an attacker-controlled `.pth` file resolves and imports successfully.
5. Module check records `model_manage_module: {ok: True, state: ready}`.
6. The overall preflight payload shows `ready: False` (due to repo_root failure), but the individual module results are misleadingly green.

## Proposed Fix

In the `check_module()` inner function, if `repo_root is None`, fail the module check immediately with a clear error message:

```python
def check_module(module_name, *, name, python_binary_path):
    try:
        if python_binary_path is None:
            raise ValueError("python_binary must be valid before module import checks")
        if repo_root is None:
            raise ValueError("repo_root must be valid before module trust boundary checks")
        checked, _origin = _resolve_importable_module(
            python_binary_path, module_name, name=name,
            trusted_root=Path(repo_root).resolve(),
            trusted_root_label="repo_root",
        )
    except Exception as exc:
        # ... existing error handling ...
```

This ensures module checks never silently bypass trusted-root enforcement.

## Acceptance Criteria

- [ ] When `repo_root` directory check fails, all subsequent module checks also report `ok: False` with a clear error message indicating the trust boundary cannot be verified.
- [ ] No module check can report `ok: True` without trusted-root containment having been verified.
- [ ] Existing tests pass; add a new test that verifies module checks fail when repo_root is invalid.

## Notes

Verify: python -m pytest tests/ops/test_ids_same_host_stack_manage.py -q
notes: Blocked pending exclusive reservation clearance for tests/ops/test_ids_same_host_stack_manage.py. Production-side fail-closed repo_root guard is already implemented in ids/ops/same_host_stack.py; remaining step is the acceptance test plus pytest verification once the shared test file is reassigned or released.
close_reason: Completed: added repo_root missing test with bypassed install

title: Add direct unit tests for _build_import_env scrubbing contract
description:
## Problem Statement

Source: review of ids_ml_new-4of7 (P2, test-coverage reviewer + direct review).

The PYTHONPATH/PYTHONHOME contamination regression tests added in bead ids_ml_new-grin (commit b456d12) have a false-confidence problem. Their docstrings claim: "If _build_import_env() stops scrubbing PYTHONPATH, the shadow module would be resolved instead." This is technically incorrect because the subprocess is also invoked with the -I flag (isolated mode), which implies -E (ignore all PYTHON* environment variables). The -I flag independently prevents PYTHONPATH/PYTHONHOME contamination.

This means if someone removes the env.pop() lines from _build_import_env(), all 4 contamination tests would still pass. The tests verify the combined defense (scrubbing + -I flag) but do not actually exercise or protect the scrubbing contract in isolation.

## Evidence

**File:** `ids/ops/module_validation.py`
**Lines:** 66-71 (`_build_import_env`) and 80-93 (`_run_module_check` with `-I` flag)

```python
def _build_import_env() -> dict[str, str]:
    env = dict(os.environ)
    env.pop("PYTHONPATH", None)   # Belt...
    env.pop("PYTHONHOME", None)
    env.pop("PYTHONSAFEPATH", None)
    return env

def _run_module_check(...):
    return subprocess.run([str(resolved_python), "-I", ...],  # ...and suspenders
        env=_build_import_env(), ...)
```

**Test files:**
- `tests/ops/test_ids_operator_console_preflight.py` lines 335-381: `test_preflight_ignores_inherited_pythonpath_contamination`, `test_preflight_ignores_inherited_pythonhome_contamination`
- `tests/ops/test_ids_same_host_stack_manage.py` lines 302-396: same pattern through stack pathway

## Concrete Failure Scenario

1. Developer reads _build_import_env() and sees the PYTHONPATH/PYTHONHOME scrubbing.
2. Developer notices the -I flag in _run_module_check() and thinks scrubbing is redundant.
3. Developer removes the env.pop() lines to "simplify" the code.
4. All 4 contamination tests still pass (because -I provides the same protection).
5. Later, a different developer removes -I for compatibility reasons.
6. Now PYTHONPATH contamination is completely undefended, and no test catches it because the scrubbing was silently removed earlier.

## Proposed Fix

Add a focused unit test for `_build_import_env()` that directly tests the returned dict:

```python
def test_build_import_env_scrubs_pythonpath(monkeypatch):
    monkeypatch.setenv("PYTHONPATH", "/evil/path")
    monkeypatch.setenv("PYTHONHOME", "/bogus/home")
    monkeypatch.setenv("PYTHONSAFEPATH", "1")
    env = _build_import_env()
    assert "PYTHONPATH" not in env
    assert "PYTHONHOME" not in env
    assert "PYTHONSAFEPATH" not in env
    # Non-PYTHON vars are preserved
    assert "PATH" in env
```

Also update the existing contamination test docstrings to accurately describe the combined defense.

## Acceptance Criteria

- [ ] A direct unit test for `_build_import_env()` asserts that PYTHONPATH, PYTHONHOME, and PYTHONSAFEPATH are absent from the returned dict when set in os.environ.
- [ ] The unit test fails if any env.pop() line is removed from `_build_import_env()`.
- [ ] Existing contamination test docstrings are updated to say they verify the combined defense (-I flag + env scrubbing), not just the scrubbing alone.

## Notes

Verify: python -m pytest tests/ops/test_ids_module_validation.py -q (new file) or whichever file the test lands in
close_reason: Completed: direct _build_import_env tests landed and contamination docstrings aligned to combined defense

title: Check all dotted segment origins against trusted root, not just the leaf
description:
## Problem Statement

Source: review of ids_ml_new-4of7 (P2, code-quality reviewer).

The _MODULE_ORIGIN_SCRIPT in ids/ops/module_validation.py walks each segment of a dotted module name (e.g., ids -> ids.console -> ids.console.server) to resolve specs without importing. However, it only prints the FINAL segment origin. The trusted-root check in resolve_importable_module() only verifies the leaf modules file is under trusted_root. Intermediate packages __init__.py origins are not checked.

This means if a shadow finder inserts a hostile ids/__init__.py or ids/console/__init__.py from OUTSIDE the trusted root, the origin check would not catch it — as long as the leaf module (server.py) resolves inside the trusted root.

## Evidence

**File:** ids/ops/module_validation.py
**Lines:** 8-42 (_MODULE_ORIGIN_SCRIPT)

```python
for index, part in enumerate(parts):
    qualified_name = part if not qualified_name else f"{qualified_name}.{part}"
    spec = _find_spec_without_import(qualified_name, search_path)
    # ... only the LAST spec origin is printed:
origin = getattr(spec, "origin", None)  # only leaf
print(json.dumps({"origin": str(Path(origin).resolve())}))
```

**Lines:** 117-125 (trusted-root check)

```python
origin_path = Path(origin).resolve()  # only leaf origin checked
if trusted_root is not None:
    origin_path.relative_to(trusted_root_path)  # only checks leaf
```

## Concrete Failure Scenario

1. Attacker installs a .pth file that adds a directory containing a hostile ids/__init__.py outside the trusted root.
2. The hostile __init__.py contains code to exfiltrate data or modify sys.path.
3. _MODULE_ORIGIN_SCRIPT resolves: ids (hostile __init__.py) -> ids.console (hostile) -> ids.console.server (legitimate, inside trusted root).
4. Only the leaf origin (server.py) is returned and checked — it passes trusted-root.
5. Import proof (step 3) runs importlib.import_module which executes the hostile __init__.py chain.

Mitigated by: -I flag limits active finders (no user site, no PYTHONPATH), so installing such a .pth requires write access to the venvs own site-packages. Low exploitability but the gap exists.

## Proposed Fix

After the atomic subprocess merge (bead ids_ml_new-fuf5.1), extend the merged script to collect origins for ALL segments and check each against trusted root:

```python
origins = []
for index, part in enumerate(parts):
    qualified_name = ...
    spec = _find_spec_without_import(qualified_name, search_path)
    seg_origin = getattr(spec, "origin", None)
    if seg_origin and seg_origin not in {"built-in", "frozen"}:
        seg_resolved = str(Path(seg_origin).resolve())
        origins.append(seg_resolved)
        if trusted_root:
            assert Path(seg_resolved).relative_to(Path(trusted_root).resolve()), \
                f"{qualified_name} origin {seg_resolved} is outside trusted root"
    # ...
# Final leaf check:
origin = origins[-1] if origins else None
```

## Acceptance Criteria

- [ ] The merged validation subprocess checks the origin of EVERY dotted segment (not just the leaf) against trusted_root.
- [ ] A test verifies that a hostile intermediate __init__.py outside the trusted root causes validation to fail, even when the leaf module is inside the trusted root.
- [ ] Existing shadow module tests still pass.

## Notes

Depends on: ids_ml_new-fuf5.1 (atomic subprocess merge — the merged script is where intermediate checks belong).
Verify: python -m pytest tests/ops/test_ids_operator_console_preflight.py tests/ops/test_ids_same_host_stack_manage.py -q
close_reason: Validated every segment origin against trusted_root

title: Add unit tests for module_validation.py error paths
description:
## Problem Statement

Source: review of ids_ml_new-4of7 (P2, test-coverage reviewer).

The newly refactored resolve_importable_module() has several distinct error paths that are only exercised through integration with the preflight and stack callers. No direct unit tests exist for the function itself. Several error paths have zero coverage:

1. origin_path.exists() check (line 119: "resolved to a missing module file") — no test
2. json.JSONDecodeError handling (line 113: "produced invalid output") — no test
3. origin being empty in the payload (line 116: "did not expose a module file") — no test
4. The trusted_root_label parameter customization — only integration-tested

## Evidence

**File:** ids/ops/module_validation.py
**Lines:** 96-129 (resolve_importable_module)

Error paths without direct coverage:
- Line 109: returncode != 0 from origin script — covered indirectly via shadow tests
- Line 113: json.JSONDecodeError — NOT covered
- Line 116: empty origin — NOT covered
- Line 119: origin file doesnt exist — NOT covered
- Line 125: origin outside trusted_root — covered via shadow tests
- Line 128: import proof fails — covered via trusted-root crash test

## Concrete Failure Scenario

A future developer "simplifies" resolve_importable_module() and accidentally removes the origin_path.exists() check (line 118-119). No test breaks because no test exercises that specific path. The removed check was a defense against race conditions between origin resolution and import proof.

## Proposed Fix

Create a focused test_ids_module_validation.py with unit tests for resolve_importable_module covering:
1. Blank module name -> ValueError
2. Non-importable module -> ValueError with "not importable" message
3. Missing origin file (mock the subprocess to return a nonexistent path) -> ValueError
4. Invalid JSON output from subprocess -> ValueError
5. Empty origin in JSON payload -> ValueError
6. Origin outside trusted root -> ValueError with trusted_root_label
7. Import proof failure (module found but crashes on import) -> ValueError
8. Happy path with and without trusted_root

These can use real subprocess calls with a tiny test module in tmp_path, or mock subprocess.run for the error injection paths.

## Acceptance Criteria

- [ ] Direct unit tests exist for resolve_importable_module() covering at minimum: blank name, non-importable module, invalid JSON output, missing origin file, empty origin, origin outside trusted_root, import proof failure, and happy path.
- [ ] Each error path test asserts the specific error message substring.
- [ ] Tests fail if the corresponding error handling code is removed.

## Notes

Depends on: ids_ml_new-fuf5.1 (test the final merged-script code shape, not the pre-refactor two-call version).
Verify: python -m pytest tests/ops/test_ids_module_validation.py -q (new file)
close_reason: Completed resolve_importable_module error-path tests

title: Unify trusted_repo_root derivation across preflight surfaces
description:
## Problem Statement

Source: review of ids_ml_new-4of7 (P2, architecture reviewer).

Two preflight surfaces derive the trusted root differently for the same security boundary:

1. operator_console_preflight.py uses _trusted_repo_root() which computes Path(__file__).resolve().parents[2] — deriving trust from the module files physical location on disk (directory depth).
2. same_host_stack.py passes config.repo_root from the SameHostStackConfig dataclass — deriving trust from explicit operator configuration.

These two patterns can diverge silently. If the file is moved, symlinked, or the directory structure changes, _trusted_repo_root() would return a different path than config.repo_root. Both are used to enforce the same trusted-root containment boundary for module validation.

## Evidence

**File:** ids/ops/operator_console_preflight.py
**Lines:** 86-87

```python
def _trusted_repo_root() -> Path:
    return Path(__file__).resolve().parents[2]
```

**File:** ids/ops/same_host_stack.py
**Lines:** 1069, 1079

```python
repo_root = check_directory(config.repo_root, name="repo_root")
# ...
trusted_root=Path(repo_root).resolve() if repo_root is not None else None,
```

## Concrete Failure Scenario

1. An operator installs the IDS stack via editable install to /opt/ids_ml_new.
2. The operator also has a symlink /opt/ids -> /opt/ids_ml_new.
3. SameHostStackConfig.repo_root is set to /opt/ids (the symlink).
4. operator_console_preflight computes _trusted_repo_root() as /opt/ids_ml_new (resolved from __file__).
5. Module origins resolve to /opt/ids_ml_new/ids/console/server.py.
6. Stack preflight checks against /opt/ids — fails because resolved origin is under /opt/ids_ml_new.
7. Console preflight checks against /opt/ids_ml_new — passes.
8. Same module, same file, different trust verdicts depending on which preflight runs.

## Proposed Fix

Make OperatorConsolePreflightConfig accept an optional repo_root parameter (defaulting to the __file__-based computation for backwards compatibility). When called from validate_stack_preflight, pass the same config.repo_root that the stack uses. When called standalone, use the __file__-based default.

```python
@dataclass(frozen=True)
class OperatorConsolePreflightConfig:
    # ... existing fields ...
    repo_root: Path | None = None  # NEW: explicit trust root, defaults to __file__ derivation

def _trusted_repo_root(config: OperatorConsolePreflightConfig) -> Path:
    if config.repo_root is not None:
        return Path(config.repo_root).resolve()
    return Path(__file__).resolve().parents[2]
```

## Acceptance Criteria

- [ ] OperatorConsolePreflightConfig accepts an optional repo_root parameter.
- [ ] When repo_root is provided, _trusted_repo_root uses it instead of __file__ derivation.
- [ ] validate_stack_preflight passes the same repo_root to the console preflight config that it uses for its own module checks.
- [ ] A test verifies that both preflight surfaces use the same trusted root when called through the stack pathway.
- [ ] Standalone console preflight (CLI) still works with the __file__-based default.

## Notes

Depends on: ids_ml_new-fuf5.2 (repo_root fail-fast should be in place first so the trust derivation is consistent with the fail-closed behavior).
Verify: python -m pytest tests/ops/test_ids_operator_console_preflight.py tests/ops/test_ids_same_host_stack_manage.py -q
close_reason: Completed: unified trusted repo root through OperatorConsolePreflightConfig

title: Include subprocess stderr in module validation error messages
description:
## Problem Statement

Source: review of ids_ml_new-4of7 (P3, direct review + security reviewer).

When module origin resolution or import proof fails, resolve_importable_module() raises ValueError with a generic message like "app_module is not importable by python_binary: ids.console.server" but discards completed.stderr, which contains the actual failure reason (assertion message, traceback, etc.).

Also, when trusted-root containment fails, the error message includes the fully resolved absolute path of the module origin, which exposes internal filesystem layout. The error should separate operator-visible summary from diagnostic detail.

## Evidence

**File:** ids/ops/module_validation.py
**Lines:** 108-109, 127-128

```python
if completed.returncode != 0:
    raise ValueError(f"{name} is not importable by python_binary: {normalized}")
    # completed.stderr is lost — contains the actual assertion message
```

## Fix

Include completed.stderr.strip() in error messages (truncated to a reasonable length). Optionally separate the operator-visible message from the diagnostic path detail.

## Acceptance Criteria

- [ ] Error messages from failed module validation include the subprocess stderr (truncated to ~500 chars if needed).
- [ ] A developer can diagnose WHY a module failed to validate from the error message alone.

## Notes

Depends on: ids_ml_new-fuf5.1 (error messages will change shape after the atomic subprocess merge).
Verify: python -m pytest tests/ops/test_ids_operator_console_preflight.py tests/ops/test_ids_same_host_stack_manage.py -q
close_reason: Verified stderr diagnostics in module validation errors

title: Broaden exception handling in _find_spec_without_import meta_path walker
description:
## Problem Statement

Source: review of ids_ml_new-4of7 (P3, direct review + code-quality reviewer).

In _MODULE_ORIGIN_SCRIPT, the _find_spec_without_import() function catches only TypeError when calling a finders find_spec(). If a meta_path finder raises a different exception (ImportError, AttributeError, or any bug in a custom finder), the origin script crashes with a generic "not importable" error instead of trying the next finder.

## Evidence

**File:** ids/ops/module_validation.py, lines 20-24 (inside _MODULE_ORIGIN_SCRIPT string)

```python
try:
    spec = method(fullname, search_path)
except TypeError:
    spec = method(fullname, search_path, None)
```

## Fix

Catch a broader exception set and continue to the next finder:

```python
try:
    spec = method(fullname, search_path)
except TypeError:
    try:
        spec = method(fullname, search_path, None)
    except Exception:
        continue
except Exception:
    continue
```

## Acceptance Criteria

- [ ] A buggy meta_path finder that raises ImportError or AttributeError does not crash the origin script; the next finder is tried.
- [ ] The TypeError retry (for finders needing 3 args) still works.

## Notes

Depends on: ids_ml_new-fuf5.1 (the script changes with the atomic merge).
Verify: python -m pytest tests/ops/test_ids_operator_console_preflight.py tests/ops/test_ids_same_host_stack_manage.py -q
close_reason: Expanded finder resilience

title: Validate Python identifier characters in clean_module_name
description:
## Problem Statement

Source: review of ids_ml_new-4of7 (P3, security reviewer).

clean_module_name() only checks that parts separated by . are non-empty after stripping. It does not validate that parts are valid Python identifiers. Characters like -, /, \, ;, unicode, etc. pass through. While subprocess.run with list args (no shell=True) prevents shell injection, and importlib.import_module would just raise ModuleNotFoundError for invalid names, tighter validation is defense-in-depth.

## Evidence

**File:** ids/ops/module_validation.py, lines 55-63

```python
def clean_module_name(value, *, name, allow_blank=True):
    # ... only checks: parts are non-empty
    if any(part.strip() == "" for part in normalized.split(".")):
        raise ValueError(...)
    return normalized
```

## Fix

Add a regex check: each dotted part must match [a-zA-Z_][a-zA-Z0-9_]*.

## Acceptance Criteria

- [ ] clean_module_name rejects names with non-identifier characters (e.g., "ids-console", "ids;evil", "ids/bad").
- [ ] Valid dotted Python module names still pass (e.g., "ids.console.server", "_private.mod").

## Notes

No dependencies. Independent change.
Verify: python -m pytest tests/ops/test_ids_operator_console_preflight.py tests/ops/test_ids_same_host_stack_manage.py -q
close_reason: Completed segment identifier validation

title: Resolve Review P2: Implement a real journald output boundary
description:
## Problem Statement

The feature boundary and docs promise local JSONL plus journald outputs, but the current sink only stores a `journald_message` string inside summary records. Formatting and transport are conflated, leaving the operational quick-inspection surface unimplemented.

## Evidence

**File:** `scripts/ids_live_sensor_sinks.py`
**Line(s):** summary rendering and persistence paths

- Reviewer evidence from architecture identified that the sink renders journald-style text but never emits it through a real logging/journald boundary.

**Why this is a problem:**
Operators cannot rely on journald as described in `D7` and the docs, and the implementation/documentation contract is mismatched.

## Proposed Solutions

### Option A — [Recommended] Add an explicit journald/stdout emission boundary
**Pros:** Aligns implementation with the promised operations surface.
**Cons:** Adds a small logging transport surface to maintain.
**Effort:** Small

### Option B — Narrow the design/docs to JSONL-only
**Pros:** Simplifies the sink boundary.
**Cons:** Changes the locked operations story and removes quick inspection via journald.
**Effort:** Small

## Acceptance Criteria

- [ ] The implementation either emits summary lines to journald/stdout explicitly, or the docs/design are narrowed to JSONL-only.
- [ ] Summary formatting is separated from transport.
- [ ] Regression coverage or packaging checks verify the chosen journald behavior.
close_reason: Completed: summary formatting and transport are separated and compact stdout lines are routed to journald

title: Resolve Review P2: Cover public batch-adaptation success paths
description:
## Problem Statement

The public batch-adaptation APIs (`StructuredRecordAdapter.adapt_records`, module-level `adapt_records`, and `BatchAdaptationResult`) currently receive only error-path coverage for missing profiles. There is no success-path verification that mixed valid/quarantined batches are bucketed correctly or that record indices propagate as intended for library callers.

## Evidence

**File:** `scripts/ids_record_adapter.py`
**Line(s):** 547, 837, 1019

**Why this is a problem:**
Public library-facing behavior can regress independently of the CLI path without test pressure.

## Proposed Solutions

### Option A — [Recommended] Add success-path batch API tests with mixed valid/quarantined records
**Pros:** Closes the library-surface gap and verifies index propagation explicitly.
**Cons:** Adds a few more adapter tests.
**Effort:** Small

### Option B — Treat the CLI as the only supported surface
**Pros:** Smaller maintenance surface.
**Cons:** Contradicts the reusable-core approach already shipped.
**Effort:** Small

## Acceptance Criteria

- [ ] Success-path tests cover `StructuredRecordAdapter.adapt_records` and module-level `adapt_records`.
- [ ] Tests verify valid/quarantine bucketing and `record_index` propagation.
- [ ] `BatchAdaptationResult` behavior is covered for library callers.
close_reason: Completed: cover mixed-success batch adaptation results and record-index propagation

title: Resolve Review P3: Clarify repo-root fallback in path-default helpers
description:
## Problem Statement

The shared path-defaults module currently exports `DEFAULT_REPO_ROOT` even though the resolver falls back to the current checkout root instead. That makes the module harder to reason about and leaves an unused production-root constant in the contract.

## Evidence

**File:** `ids/core/path_defaults.py`

**Why this is a problem:**
The path-default helper is supposed to centralize behavior, but one of its key constants is currently misleading dead configuration.

## Proposed Solutions

### Option A — [Recommended] Remove or rename the unused constant and cover the actual resolver behavior directly
**Pros:** Makes intent explicit and avoids false assumptions.
**Cons:** Small cleanup only.
**Effort:** Small

### Option B — Change resolver fallback to use the exported constant
**Pros:** Aligns declaration and behavior.
**Cons:** Could break editable-checkout assumptions if done carelessly.
**Effort:** Medium

## Acceptance Criteria

- [ ] The path-default helper has one unambiguous repo-root fallback story.
- [ ] Direct tests cover unset/set `IDS_REPO_ROOT` resolution behavior.
close_reason: Resolved during fresh-eyes review fix pass

title: Spike: Can the operator console runtime model reverse-proxied HTTPS safely with explicit public_base_url?
description:
Question to answer:
Can the current FastAPI + Starlette SessionMiddleware + uvicorn stack, as used in this repo, support a same-host internal bind behind a trusted reverse proxy while keeping redirects, cookie security posture, and generated paths/origins correct through an explicit public_base_url + proxy-header contract?

Why this spike exists:
Approach.md marks proxy-aware config + session hardening as HIGH risk. The current runtime uses https_only=False, no proxy/header config, and no public origin contract.

Scope:
- inspect current operator-console runtime wiring (config.py, web.py, ids_operator_console_server.py, tests)
- verify official FastAPI / uvicorn behavior for proxy headers and root-path style deployment
- determine the minimum production-safe contract for this repo

Output:
- write FINDINGS.md under .spikes/ids-operator-console-production-hardening/<spike-id>/
- conclude YES or NO
- if YES, list exact constraints that downstream beads must follow
- if NO, describe the blocker precisely

Success bar for YES:
- trusted-proxy assumptions are concrete
- public_base_url/root-path implications are explicit
- session-cookie hardening has a fail-closed path that fits this stack
close_reason: YES: proxy-aware runtime is viable if execution adds explicit forwarded_allow_ips/root_path/public_base_url config, sets secure session cookies fail-closed, and keeps proxy/header assumptions explicit.

title: Resolve Review P2: Expand extractor and bridge regression coverage
description:
## Source Epic
- ids_ml_new-vii9

## Problem
The new test suite proves the happy-path extractor contract and a single-row bridge integration, but it leaves several parser, flow-classification, and multi-row handling branches unprotected.

## Evidence
- 	ests/test_ids_offline_window_extractor.py covers one synthetic bidirectional TCP window and a golden CSV fixture only.
- Branches for malformed/truncated pcap, unsupported magic/link types, UDP/VLAN decoding, non-IP filtering, backward-first flow merging, and multi-flow aggregation remain untested.
- 	ests/test_ids_live_flow_bridge.py proves one successful replacement-extractor row, but not multiple rows with mixed adapted/quarantine outcomes.

## Why It Matters
This extractor boundary emits degraded/quarantine states and model-facing numeric features. Happy-path coverage alone is not enough to prevent silent regressions.

## Acceptance Criteria
- Add negative fixtures for malformed/truncated pcap and unsupported formats.
- Add targeted extractor tests for UDP/VLAN, non-IP filtering, reverse-first flow aggregation, and sub-second duration math.
- Add bridge integration coverage for multiple output rows, including preserved ordering and mixed adapted/quarantine results.
close_reason: Completed: expanded extractor and bridge regression coverage

title: Resolve Review P2: Bound hostile JSONL input before decoding
description:
## Problem Statement

The adapter decodes each JSONL line with no size or structure guardrail before parsing. That means hostile oversized lines can force expensive decode work before quarantine, turning malformed input into an availability problem for the ingestion path.

## Evidence

**File:** `scripts/ids_record_adapter.py`
**Line(s):** 520-526

The read loop strips each line and immediately calls `json.loads` without a size check.

## Proposed Solutions

### Option A — [Recommended] Add a maximum line-size guard and quarantine oversized inputs
**Pros:** Adds a bounded failure mode for hostile or accidental huge records.
**Cons:** Requires choosing and documenting an operational limit.
**Effort:** Small

### Option B — Leave parsing unbounded and rely on process-level limits
**Pros:** No code change.
**Cons:** Leaves availability dependent on deployment-specific safeguards.
**Effort:** Small

## Acceptance Criteria

- [ ] Oversized or pathological JSONL lines are rejected or quarantined before `json.loads` does unbounded work.
- [ ] The adapter emits a clear quarantine reason for over-limit records.
- [ ] Tests cover the new size guard behavior.
close_reason: Completed: added a bounded oversized-line quarantine guard before JSON decode

title: Harden repo-installable proof against ambient site-packages leaks
description: Update tests/ops/test_ids_repo_installable_bootstrap_proof.py and tests/ops/test_ids_repo_installable_surface.py so the repo-installable proof no longer inherits global site packages. Keep the D4 proof centered on an isolated fresh-host-style editable install that executes installed console scripts directly. In this bead, wrapper-first execution means proofs that invoke scripts/* shims or python -m scripts.* as the primary packaged path instead of the installed ids-* console scripts.
acceptance_criteria: Packaging-proof venv helpers no longer use --system-site-packages. The isolated proof still installs the repo with pip install -e . and exercises the installed canonical command surface through installed ids-* console scripts, not through scripts/* wrappers or python -m scripts.* entry modes. Verify: python -m pytest tests/ops/test_ids_repo_installable_bootstrap_proof.py tests/ops/test_ids_repo_installable_surface.py -q exits 0.
close_reason: Completed: isolated editable install proof now uses a fresh venv and declared runtime deps

title: Resolve Review P2: Re-add caller-environment contamination regressions for module validation
description:
## Problem Statement

Source epic: `ids_ml_new-urzq`.

The hermetic-import regressions added in the review-fix wave cover clean-venv failure and shadow modules injected through `sitecustomize`, but they no longer exercise the original hostile inherited-environment seam that the wave was supposed to close. If `_build_import_env()` stops scrubbing `PYTHONPATH` or `PYTHONHOME` again, the current tests can remain green while the caller-environment leak quietly returns.

## Evidence

**File:** `ids/ops/module_validation.py`
**Line(s):** 20-24

```python
def _build_import_env() -> dict[str, str]:
    env = dict(os.environ)
    env.pop(PYTHONPATH, None)
    env.pop(PYTHONHOME, None)
    env.pop(PYTHONSAFEPATH, None)
    return env
```

**File:** `tests/ops/test_ids_operator_console_preflight.py`
**Line(s):** 221-238

```python
def test_preflight_rejects_shadowed_module(...):
    ...
    monkeypatch.setenv(IDS_TEST_SHADOW_IMPORT_MODULE, shadow_module)
    monkeypatch.setenv(IDS_TEST_SHADOW_IMPORT_ROOT, str(shadow_root))
```

**Why this is a problem:** the tests no longer pin the explicit caller-environment contamination contract that motivated the scrubber in the first place.

## Proposed Solutions

### Option A - Recommended: add explicit inherited-env regressions
**Pros:** directly protects the seam this review-fix wave was opened to close.
**Cons:** requires a little more test harness setup.
**Effort:** Small

### Option B - rely on clean-venv and sitecustomize coverage only
**Pros:** no new tests.
**Cons:** leaves the original PYTHONPATH/PYTHONHOME regression path unguarded.
**Effort:** None

## Acceptance Criteria

- [ ] Regression coverage proves that inherited `PYTHONPATH` contamination does not influence packaged module validation.
- [ ] Regression coverage proves that inherited `PYTHONHOME` contamination does not influence packaged module validation.
- [ ] The new tests fail if `_build_import_env()` stops scrubbing those caller-environment variables.
notes: Verify: python -m pytest tests/ops/test_ids_operator_console_preflight.py tests/ops/test_ids_same_host_stack_manage.py -q
close_reason: Completed: caller-environment contamination regressions added

title: Resolve Review P2: Normalize corrupted backup metadata JSON failures
description:
## Problem Statement

Backup restore metadata parsing still lets malformed JSON escape as raw JSON decode failures instead of surfacing as `OpsError`. Restore already treats integrity and manifest checks as operator-facing contracts, so corrupted metadata must fail through the same controlled error path before any live swap logic proceeds.

Source epic traceability: `ids_ml_new-br4g` fresh reviewing rerun.

## Evidence

**File:** `ids/console/ops.py`

- `_load_manifest()` and `_load_backup_integrity()` call `json.loads()` directly on untrusted files.

## Acceptance Criteria

- [ ] Malformed backup manifest JSON is surfaced as `OpsError`.
- [ ] Malformed backup integrity JSON is surfaced as `OpsError`.
- [ ] Regression tests prove corrupted metadata fails closed before any live DB replacement.
close_reason: Completed: corrupted backup manifest/integrity JSON now fails through OpsError with regression coverage

title: IDS model bundle promotion hardening
description:
Production-hardening epic for the single-host IDS model bundle lifecycle.

Scope:
- versioned bundle contract and compatibility metadata
- explicit verify/promote/activate/rollback workflow
- fail-closed runtime + preflight wiring to the active bundle contract
- active bundle visibility in summaries/readiness/operator console
- same-host runbooks and regression coverage

Locked decisions: D1-D16 in history/ids-model-bundle-promotion-hardening/CONTEXT.md
close_reason: Completed and reviewed: model bundle promotion hardening delivered end-to-end

title: Implement versioned bundle contract and active bundle resolver
description:
Implement the canonical versioned model-bundle contract and active-bundle resolver.

File scope:
- scripts/ids_model_bundle.py
- scripts/package_final_model.py
- scripts/ids_inference.py
- artifacts/final_model/catboost_full_data_v1/model_bundle.json (fixture/reference handling as needed)
- tests/test_ids_model_bundle.py
- tests/test_ids_inference.py

Context from planning:
- Recommended approach: centralize manifest validation, compatibility checks, and active-state resolution in one lifecycle module.
- Locked decisions: D4, D5, D6, D7.

Institutional learnings:
- history/learnings/20260328-adapter-rollback-contract.md: rollback/restore must stay on atomic replace only; no copy fallback.
- history/learnings/20260329-operator-console-production-hardening.md: runtime verify-only, explicit operator mutation path.

Verification criteria:
- manifest version + compatibility metadata are validated explicitly
- active bundle can be resolved from the canonical activation contract
- mismatched schema/config/contract cases fail closed in tests
- inference config resolution no longer permits production drift between bundle and external model/schema/threshold inputs
notes:
Validated constraints:
- ids_ml_new-tz9 (YES): evolve the existing model_bundle.json in place; add manifest_version plus a compatibility block while preserving current core fields during migration.
- ids_ml_new-a8t (YES): keep activation state separate from bundle contents and resolve the active bundle through an atomic replace-managed activation record.
Execution guardrails:
- fail closed when compatibility metadata is missing or mismatched.
- do not introduce any copy-based restore logic in the activation path.
close_reason: Completed: versioned bundle contract validation and active-bundle resolver added

title: Add explicit bundle manage CLI for verify promote rollback
description:
Add the explicit same-host operator CLI for bundle lifecycle actions: inspect, verify candidate, promote/activate, rollback, and status.

File scope:
- scripts/ids_model_bundle_manage.py
- scripts/ids_model_bundle.py
- tests/test_ids_model_bundle_manage.py
- tests/test_ids_model_bundle.py

Context from planning:
- Recommended approach: keep all model-lifecycle mutation out of runtime startup and behind explicit operator commands.
- Locked decisions: D3, D8, D9, D10, D11.

Institutional learnings:
- scripts/ids_operator_console_manage.py is the mutation CLI pattern to follow.
- history/learnings/20260328-adapter-rollback-contract.md applies to activation/rollback implementation.

Verification criteria:
- CLI supports dry-run or equivalent pre-activation verification path
- promote records previous-known-good state before cutover
- rollback returns to the recorded previous bundle without copy-based restore logic
- CLI-level tests cover success and failure paths
notes:
Validated constraints:
- ids_ml_new-a8t (YES): the CLI owns mutation through the activation record, records the previous known-good bundle before cutover, and rollback rewrites activation state rather than copying bundle directories.
- ids_ml_new-d85 (YES): verify/promote/rollback output must reflect the same active-bundle contract consumed by runtime and preflight.
Execution guardrails:
- keep the verification path same-host only (dry-run/smoke), not multi-host rollout.
close_reason: Completed: explicit bundle manage CLI added for verify promote rollback status

title: Wire live sensor runtime and preflight to active bundle contract

Wire live sensor runtime, preflight, and deploy artifacts to the canonical active bundle contract.

- scripts/ids_live_sensor_preflight.py

- tests/test_ids_live_sensor.py
- tests/test_ids_live_sensor_preflight.py

Context from planning:
- Recommended approach: production runtime and preflight must resolve the active bundle through one canonical contract and fail closed on incompatibility.
- Locked decisions: D5, D7, D8, D13.

Institutional learnings:
- history/learnings/20260328-live-sensor-runtime-contracts.md: exact-path preflight and one config source.
- history/learnings/20260329-operator-console-production-hardening.md: startup stays verify-only.

Verification criteria:
- runtime no longer relies on split production model/schema path inputs
- preflight validates the same active bundle contract that runtime consumes
- systemd unit wiring matches the new contract
- tests cover incompatible or missing active bundle failure modes
notes:
Validated constraints:
- ids_ml_new-d85 (YES): runtime, preflight, and systemd must share one active-bundle input; remove split production model/schema/threshold env wiring from the service contract.
- ids_ml_new-tz9 (YES): runtime resolves compatibility metadata from the versioned bundle manifest, not from ad hoc external overrides.
Execution guardrails:
- startup remains verify-only and fails closed when the active bundle is missing or incompatible.
close_reason: Completed: runtime preflight and systemd now resolve the active bundle contract

comments.text: Reopened: Review found blocking follow-up work

title: Resolve Review P1: Build release bundles from a safe export surface
description:
## Plain-Language Summary

The release helper currently packages the live working tree with a short manual exclude list. That means local, untracked files that were never meant to ship can still end up inside the deployment tarball.

## What The Code Does Today

- `ops/build_release.sh` builds the release bundle by running `tar -cf - .` from the repo root.
- The script excludes a few known directories, but it is not driven by git tracking state or `.gitignore`.
- This feature also adds `.claude` to `.gitignore`, which shows there are local-only files in the repo surface that should not ship.

## Why This Is A Problem

- A release artifact should contain the intended product, not whatever happened to exist in one engineer's checkout at build time.
- If a build host has local secrets, credentials, or private review artifacts in untracked files, they can be copied into the tarball and then propagated to a target host or artifact store.
- This is a real security boundary failure, not just release noise.

## Concrete Failure Scenario

- A build host has an untracked directory such as `.claude/` or another local file with credentials.
- An operator runs `bash ./ops/build_release.sh`.
- The script archives the repo root and silently includes that local file.
- The resulting `ids_ml_new-*.tar.gz` is uploaded or copied to another host.
- Anyone with access to the artifact can extract the leaked material.

## Evidence

**File:** `ops/build_release.sh`
**Line(s):** 75-98

The script creates the bundle with a manual tar exclude list and archives `.` from the repository root instead of exporting an ignore-aware tracked file set.

**File:** `.gitignore`
**Line(s):** 32-35

The diff adds `.claude` to ignored local files, but `ops/build_release.sh` does not exclude it.

## Proposed Solutions

### Option A — Recommended: build from a git-tracked export surface
**Pros:** prevents ignored and untracked local files from shipping by construction.
**Cons:** requires changing the staging logic.
**Effort:** Medium

### Option B — expand the manual exclude list
**Pros:** smaller patch.
**Cons:** brittle and easy to regress when a new local-only directory appears.
**Effort:** Small

## Acceptance Criteria

- [ ] The release bundle is built from a tracked/allowlisted export surface rather than the raw working tree.
- [ ] Ignored and untracked local files cannot be included in the tarball.
- [ ] Regression coverage proves a local ignored file is absent from the produced archive.
notes:
## Phase Context

Phase: Phase 4 - Review closure and release-proof hardening
What Changes: the release artifact becomes safe to ship instead of reflecting whatever happened to be in one checkout.
Unlocks Next: the rest of the review-closure phase can rely on a trustworthy shipped surface.

## Story Context

Story: Story 1 - Repair the shipped deploy surface
What Happens: the tarball stops using the raw working tree as the product boundary.
Contributes To: the exit-state requirement that the shipped artifact is safe and intentional.
Unlocks: installer/runtime closure work and final closure proof.

## Planning Context

From approach.md: choose a safe export surface by construction, not a longer manual exclude list.

## Institutional Learnings

From history/learnings/20260403-packaging-contract-proof.md:
- packaging closure must prove the real shipped artifact surface rather than trusting a warm tree.

## File Scope

Primary files:
- ops/build_release.sh
- any helper or staging logic directly required by that script

Secondary files only if truly necessary:
- tests/ops/test_deploy_helper_contract.py

## Verification Criteria

- The archive is built from a tracked or allowlisted export surface.
- Ignored and untracked local files are excluded by construction.
- Any shared contract proof changes are coordinated with ids_ml_new-i7oa.2.

## Validation Spike Result

Spike ids_ml_new-i7oa.3: YES.
Constraints:
- stay inside the existing tarball plus install.sh model from D4
- treat `wheelhouse/` as the only acceptable generated post-export artifact
- do not rely on a longer manual exclude list as the primary safety mechanism
close_reason: Completed by WhiteMill, verified by CrimsonCrane (3/3 deploy contract tests pass, git archive export surface confirmed)

title: Phase 4 / Story 3: Prove closure and align deploy docs
description:
## Plain-Language Summary

The review follow-up only becomes truly closed when the repaired deploy surface, the unified Telegram config contract, and the root-path-aware settings flow are all proven together and reflected in the operator docs. Without that last proof pass, the phase can still look fixed file-by-file while the shipped contract remains ambiguous.

## Phase Context

Phase: Phase 4 - Review closure and release-proof hardening
What Changes: the feature becomes genuinely shippable instead of merely mostly implemented.
Unlocks Next: final validation approval for execution and then a real closure review.

## Story Context

Story: Story 3 - Make the settings surface work in real deployed topology and prove closure
What Happens: final contract-oriented proof is added after the deploy-surface fixes, config-truth fixes, and root-path fixes are in place.
Contributes To: the phase exit state that says the artifact is safe, the UI tells the truth, mounted deployments work, and install/runtime closure is real.
Unlocks: a final review pass that can verify closure instead of reopening the same seams.

## Planning Context

From approach.md: closure proof for this phase must be explicit and artifact-real, not just a warmed-worktree confidence check. Docs must match the corrected release/install/runtime story.

## Institutional Learnings

From history/learnings/20260403-packaging-contract-proof.md:
- prove the shipped artifact surface in a scrubbed, contract-oriented way rather than trusting a warm developer tree.

From history/learnings/20260329-operator-console-production-hardening.md:
- proxy-facing and deploy-facing behavior should be documented against the same contract runtime actually enforces.

## File Scope

Primary files:
- tests/ops/test_deploy_helper_contract.py
- docs/current/operations/deployment_quickstart.md
- docs/current/operations/README.md

Avoid touching implementation files already owned by earlier Phase 4 beads unless proof gaps force it.

## Verification Criteria

- Add or tighten contract tests that prove the repaired release/export and install/runtime surfaces hold together.
- Update operator docs so the final documented flow matches the corrected artifact, install, and Telegram-settings behavior.
- Leave a validation-ready proof surface for reviewers instead of relying on manual interpretation.
