title: Resolve Review P2: Normalize live extractor deployment contract
description:
## Problem
Review found the live extractor deployment seam is inconsistent across systemd, preflight, and same-host stack surfaces. The current code models extractor_command_prefix as a multi-token argv contract, but the shipped systemd unit passes it as a single quoted environment expansion, which collapses multi-token prefixes at runtime. In parallel, preflight and stack code still expose overlapping legacy fields (java_binary, extractor_binary, jnetpcap_path) alongside the new prefix surface, leaving two public APIs for the same dependency seam.

## Evidence
- deploy/systemd/ids-live-sensor.service quotes IDS_LIVE_SENSOR_EXTRACTOR_COMMAND_PREFIX inside ExecStart, so multi-token prefixes are not preserved.
- scripts/ids_live_sensor_preflight.py still carries legacy extractor dependency fields with hidden fallback behavior.
- scripts/ids_same_host_stack.py and scripts/ids_same_host_stack_manage.py continue to propagate the split contract.

## Why It Matters
This is a real deployment correctness gap for replacement extractors that need more than one argv token, and it keeps the runtime dependency contract harder to reason about than the feature intended.

## Acceptance Criteria
- The live service path preserves multi-token extractor prefixes exactly as configured, or the contract is narrowed explicitly to single-token prefixes and docs/tests are updated to match.
- Preflight and same-host stack expose one canonical extractor dependency surface, with any legacy fields isolated behind an explicit compatibility shim or removed.
- Regression coverage proves the shipped contract matches the implemented contract.
close_reason: Completed: preserve multi-token extractor prefixes end-to-end and remove legacy preflight dependency fields

title: Harden operator console notification runtime
description:
Feature goal: harden the operator console's outbound Telegram notification path into a same-host runtime contract that is production-ready, operator-visible, restart-safe, and isolated from core console readiness.

Verification criteria:
- planning artifacts exist and align with history/ids-operator-console-notification-runtime-hardening/CONTEXT.md
- validating can spike the HIGH-risk items before any execution begins
- bead graph reflects runtime-core -> operator-surface -> deploy/docs dependency order
close_reason: Execution complete: notification runtime hardening beads implemented and verified

title: Implement notification maintenance runtime core
description:
Implement the notification maintenance runtime core that turns existing notification primitives into an explicit same-host worker contract.

What / Why:
- Add a focused orchestration seam that must own the full maintenance cycle outside the web app.
- Reuse existing primitives for ingest refresh, suppression-aware queueing, dispatch/retry, and persisted delivery state.
- Make disabled-mode, retry visibility, and redrive semantics explicit enough for operators and downstream health surfaces.

File scope:
- scripts/ids_operator_console/notification_runtime.py (new)
- scripts/ids_operator_console/notifications.py
- scripts/ids_operator_console/db.py
- scripts/ids_operator_console/ingest.py
- tests/test_ids_operator_console_notification_runtime.py (new)
- tests/test_ids_operator_console_notifications.py
- tests/test_ids_operator_console_db.py
- tests/test_ids_operator_console_ingest.py

Verification criteria:
- a run-once maintenance cycle refreshes alerts first, queues candidates second, and dispatches due deliveries third in the chosen order
- disabled Telegram mode is explicit and does not silently create unbounded pending backlog
- persisted retry/failed state remains restart-safe and supports operator-visible redrive semantics
- targeted pytest coverage proves failure isolation at the runtime-core layer

Context from Planning:
- Follow D3-D10 and D16 from history/ids-operator-console-notification-runtime-hardening/CONTEXT.md.
- The worker must stay outside ids_operator_console_server.py, must treat SQLite notification_deliveries as the local source of truth, and must own the full ingest -> queue -> dispatch cycle rather than dispatch alone.

Institutional Learnings:
- history/learnings/20260328-operator-console-runtime-wiring.md: prove wired runtime paths, not only module presence.
- history/learnings/20260329-operator-console-production-hardening.md: keep maintenance work out of verify-only web startup.
- history/learnings/20260328-live-sensor-runtime-contracts.md: durable runtime evidence and clear failure boundaries matter more than shutdown-time success.
acceptance_criteria: Verification command: python -m pytest -q tests/test_ids_operator_console_notification_runtime.py tests/test_ids_operator_console_notifications.py tests/test_ids_operator_console_db.py tests/test_ids_operator_console_ingest.py exits 0. The implementation must prove ordered ingest, queue, dispatch behavior plus restart-safe retry and redrive state.
notes: Validated by spike ids_ml_new-xpl: runtime is feasible if execution adds a dedicated orchestration seam that runs ingest refresh before queue and dispatch, keeps disabled Telegram from growing silent backlog, and treats dispatch errors as notification-local degradation only.
close_reason: Completed: add notification runtime orchestration and explicit redrive-safe delivery state

title: Resolve Review P1: Make notification worker a real long-running supervised runtime
description:
## Problem Statement

The shipped notification worker is not actually a durable same-host worker. The new service unit and CLI both run the worker for a single iteration and then exit cleanly, which means the runtime stops after one maintenance cycle instead of continuously owning queue/dispatch/retry.

## Evidence

**Files:**
- `deploy/systemd/ids-operator-console-notify.service`
- `scripts/ids_operator_console_manage.py`
- `scripts/ids_operator_console/ops.py`

**Why this is a problem:**
- `deploy/systemd/ids-operator-console-notify.service` hardcodes `notify-worker --iterations 1 --poll-interval-seconds 30` and only restarts on failure, so a successful cycle leaves the unit stopped.
- `scripts/ids_operator_console_manage.py` defaults `notify-worker` to `--iterations 1`, reinforcing one-shot behavior instead of a persistent supervised worker contract.
- `scripts/ids_operator_console/ops.py` currently passes a no-op sleep function into the worker wrapper, so even multi-iteration runs do not honor the configured poll interval.

This violates the feature contract from D3, D4, D12, and D16: the notification path was required to have an explicit long-running worker mode that can really own dispatch/retry on the same host without being embedded in the web process.

## Proposed Solutions

### Option A — Recommended: make `notify-worker` genuinely long-running by default
**Pros:** Restores the promised runtime ownership model and matches the new systemd worker unit.
**Cons:** Requires tightening CLI/runtime semantics and test coverage around looping behavior.
**Effort:** Medium

### Option B — explicitly switch the deployment model to timer/oneshot and rewrite docs/contract
**Pros:** Smaller runtime loop surface.
**Cons:** Contradicts the already-shipped worker service and current locked decisions unless the full contract is reworked.
**Effort:** Medium

## Acceptance Criteria

- [ ] `notify-worker` has a true long-running mode suitable for supervision, rather than defaulting to one-shot behavior.
- [ ] The configured poll interval is actually honored between iterations.
- [ ] `ids-operator-console-notify.service` stays alive as the notification owner under normal operation instead of exiting after one clean pass.
- [ ] Targeted tests prove long-running worker behavior and poll/sleep semantics, not only bounded one-shot execution.
close_reason: Review fix pass verified: notify-worker now runs supervised by default and honors poll cadence

title: Resolve Review P2: Keep repo-installable metadata and canonical entrypoint surface explicit
description:
## Problem Statement

The current review-followup wave assumes the repo-installable surface stays correct while follow-up fixes land, but no bead explicitly owns the installed metadata contract itself. That leaves `pyproject.toml`, console-script registration, package-data inclusion, and the single canonical operator-facing install/docs spine under-owned even though validator expects those seams to stay explicit for D3, D6, and D9.

## Evidence

**Files:** `pyproject.toml`, `tests/ops/test_ids_repo_installable_surface.py`, `tests/docs/test_docs_path_smoke.py`, `docs/current/operations/ids_same_host_stack_operations.md`, `docs/current/runtime/final_model_bundle.md`

**Why this is a problem:**
If follow-up fixes mutate runtime, packaging, or docs without one bead owning the installed surface contract, validating cannot prove that canonical entrypoints still map directly to `ids/*` modules, package data still ships correctly, and operators still have one canonical install/bootstrap story.

## Proposed Solutions

### Option A ? [Recommended] Make the installed surface an explicit follow-up owner
**Pros:** Restores clear ownership for install metadata, canonical entrypoint mapping, and the operator-facing command spine.
**Cons:** Adds one more bead to the wave.
**Effort:** Small

### Option B ? keep the installed surface implicit inside other follow-up beads
**Pros:** Less immediate graph churn.
**Cons:** Repeats the exact ambiguity that blocked validation.
**Effort:** Small

## Acceptance Criteria

- [ ] `pyproject.toml` remains the canonical install metadata surface for this feature wave, with installed entrypoints mapped directly to canonical `ids/*` modules (and the approved `ml_pipeline.packaging.package_final_model` command), never to `scripts/*` wrappers.
- [ ] Package-data and operator-facing docs still describe one canonical repo-installable command surface rather than multiple equivalent install paths.
- [ ] Regression coverage proves the install metadata, entrypoint map, and docs command spine remain aligned after the follow-up fixes.

> KNOWN PATTERN: Matches 20260331-repo-structure-wrapper-contracts.md.
> Previous occurrence: `history/learnings/20260331-repo-structure-wrapper-contracts.md`
> Past resolution: preserve wrappers as compatibility contracts, but keep canonical package metadata and canonical entrypoints explicit and directly tested.
notes: Verify: python -m pytest tests/ops/test_ids_repo_installable_surface.py tests/docs/test_docs_path_smoke.py -q
close_reason: Completed: verified repo-installable surface and docs smoke already pass

title: IDS same-host stack runtime hardening
description:
Feature goal: harden the IDS same-host stack into one explicit Linux host contract that can bootstrap, preflight, smoke, recover, and verify post-restore across model activation, live sensor, operator console, notification worker, and optional reverse proxy seam.

Locked decisions:
- D1-D3: stack layer is thin and delegates to existing component owners
- D5-D7: top-level health gates on activation + sensor + console + notification when enabled; proxy is non-gating and failure domains stay explicit
- D9-D15: stack bootstrap, preflight, recovery, and post-restore verification must be canonical and real

Verification:
- discovery.md and approach.md exist for this feature
- all child tasks close with shipped artifacts and tests/docs aligned to the final stack contract
- validating spikes HIGH-risk items before any execution begins
close_reason: Swarm execution complete: all child beads closed and final stack verification passed

title: Implement live sensor health seam for stack status
description:
What:
Implement a read-only live sensor health/status seam that the stack manager can trust for stack-level `status` and `smoke`.

Why:
This is a HIGH-risk assumption in approach.md. The repo has sensor preflight and daemon runtime, but no canonical runtime status surface today.

How:
- add the smallest possible sensor-domain helper (for example `scripts/ids_live_sensor_health.py` or equivalent narrow module)
- reuse activation-record semantics and durable summary evidence instead of inventing a second source of truth
- keep the helper read-only; no mutation, no service control, no ownership drift away from `ids_live_sensor.py` + systemd

Context from Planning:
- Approach section 2: add one narrow read-only sensor health seam
- Locked decisions D1-D3, D5, D7, D12-D15 apply directly

Institutional Learnings:
- `history/learnings/20260328-live-sensor-runtime-contracts.md`: use durable runtime evidence and exact-path contracts; do not rely on shutdown-only publication or vague runtime guesses
- `history/learnings/20260329-model-bundle-promotion-hardening.md`: keep production model selection on one activation contract

File Scope:
- scripts/ids_live_sensor_health.py (or equivalent narrow helper)
- tests/test_ids_live_sensor_health.py
- minimal supporting imports in existing live sensor modules only if necessary

Verification Criteria:
- `python -m pytest -q tests/test_ids_live_sensor_health.py`
- expected: pass cases prove healthy, stale/missing evidence, and incompatible activation/evidence paths
- helper stays machine-readable and introduces no startup mutation or service-control side effects
close_reason: Completed: added read-only live sensor health payload over activation + durable summary evidence

title: Implement stack bootstrap and host-layout preflight contract
description:
What:
Implement the canonical stack bootstrap and host-layout preflight contract in the thin stack manager.

Why:
Plan-checker flagged missing explicit ownership for D9 and D11. The feature needs a shipped stack bootstrap path plus one canonical check for `/opt`, `/var/lib`, `/var/log`, env files, and secret-file references.

How:
- implement stack-manager entrypoints for fresh-host bootstrap ordering and stack preflight
- compose existing component contracts instead of re-owning mutation logic
- verify host layout/config seams explicitly across activation path, console DB path, env file, secret files, and required writable roots
- keep reverse proxy non-gating and outside top-level readiness

Context from Planning:
- Locked decisions D1-D3, D9-D11 are non-negotiable
- This bead owns canonical bootstrap ordering and host-layout/config seam verification

Institutional Learnings:
- `history/learnings/20260328-live-sensor-runtime-contracts.md`: exact-path preflight and one config source
- `history/learnings/20260329-operator-console-production-hardening.md`: keep runtime verification separate from mutation and wire secret/preflight contracts consistently

File Scope:

- scripts/ids_same_host_stack_manage.py
- tests/test_ids_same_host_stack_manage.py

Verification Criteria:

- stack preflight remains verification-oriented and does not absorb component-specific mutation logic

title: Implement stack restore inventory and post-restore verification
description:
What:
Implement stack restore inventory and post-restore verification for the minimum same-host restore inventory.

Why:
Plan-checker flagged missing ownership for D13-D15 and D14. The feature needs a real contract for restore-state inventory, post-restore verification, and evidence-preservation posture.

How:
- implement stack-manager checks for restore inventory covering active_bundle.json, referenced bundle roots, operator_console.db, and restore manifests/secret references
- implement post-restore verification that re-validates active bundle, console smoke, notification status/redrive when enabled, and bundle visibility re-establishment
- encode the evidence-preservation posture for live-sensor JSONL/log outputs as preserve-when-practical, non-primary restore state
- keep restore mutations owned by existing component-specific commands unless validating later proves a wrapper is justified

Context from Planning:
- Locked decisions D13-D15 are non-negotiable
- This bead owns the restore inventory/evidence contract and post-restore verification path after the canonical restart/recovery flow exists

Institutional Learnings:
- history/learnings/20260329-model-bundle-promotion-hardening.md: failed mutations preserve known-good state and restore must preserve operator visibility semantics
- history/learnings/20260329-notification-runtime-contracts.md: restore verification must include notification status/redrive, not only restored rows existing

File Scope:
- scripts/ids_same_host_stack.py
- scripts/ids_same_host_stack_manage.py
- tests/test_ids_same_host_stack_manage.py

Verification Criteria:
- python -m pytest -q tests/test_ids_same_host_stack_manage.py -k restore_or_post_restore
- expected: pass cases prove restore inventory checks, post-restore verification, notification redrive/status handling, and evidence-preservation posture
- no component-specific restore mutation logic is duplicated in the stack layer
close_reason: Completed: added restore inventory and post-restore verification

title: Implement stack status and smoke aggregation
description:
What:
Implement canonical stack `status` and `smoke` aggregation in the thin stack manager.

Why:
The feature needs one stack-level operational verdict that stays explicit per failure domain and reuses existing component contracts.

How:
- aggregate model activation status, live sensor health seam, console smoke/readiness, notification status, and optional proxy seam into one machine-readable payload
- model top-level health exactly per D5-D7
- keep proxy optional/non-gating and notification gating only when enabled
- do not infer component state from undocumented heuristics

Context from Planning:
- Locked decisions D4-D8 are non-negotiable
- Depends on sensor health seam and stack bootstrap/preflight surfaces being in place

Institutional Learnings:
- `history/learnings/20260328-operator-console-runtime-wiring.md`: `EXISTS / SUBSTANTIVE / WIRED` must include deploy/runtime entrypoints
- `history/learnings/20260329-notification-runtime-contracts.md`: keep notification state explicit and separate from web/runtime ownership

File Scope:
- scripts/ids_same_host_stack.py
- scripts/ids_same_host_stack_manage.py
- tests/test_ids_same_host_stack_manage.py
- deploy/nginx/ids-operator-console.conf.example only if stack seam inputs require documented proxy wiring

Verification Criteria:
- `python -m pytest -q tests/test_ids_same_host_stack_manage.py -k status_or_smoke`
- expected: pass cases prove top-level health semantics across notifications enabled/disabled/degraded and proxy configured/unconfigured cases
- stack payload reports model activation, live sensor, operator visibility, outbound notification, and proxy as separate failure domains
close_reason: Completed: added canonical stack status and smoke aggregation with explicit failure domains

title: Publish same-host stack runbook and deploy references

Why:

How:

- update deploy references only where the final stack contract requires them

Institutional Learnings:

title: Resolve Review P2: Add end-to-end coverage for migrated ML workflows
description:
## Problem Statement

The migrated ML workflow surfaces now have wrapper/help smoke, but the substantial end-to-end workflow paths are still not exercised. That leaves dataset staging, packaging, training, and threshold-analysis mains vulnerable to regressions that the current suite would miss.

Source epic traceability: `ids_ml_new-br4g` fresh reviewing rerun.

## Evidence

**Files:** `tests/ml/test_ml_workflow_wrapper_smoke.py`, `ml_pipeline/data_prep/preprocess_iot_diad.py`, `ml_pipeline/packaging/package_final_model.py`, `ml_pipeline/training/train_iot_diad_binary.py`, `ml_pipeline/training/posttrain_threshold_analysis.py`

## Acceptance Criteria

- [ ] Add fixture-based integration coverage for the main migrated ML workflow families.
- [ ] Each covered workflow asserts a real success-path artifact/output in a temp directory.
- [ ] At least one failure-path branch is covered for each workflow family added.
close_reason: Completed: added end-to-end ML workflow coverage for preprocess, package, train, and posttrain analysis with failure-path checks

title: Spike: Can the current bead split keep shared CSS and shell file scopes conflict-free?
close_reason: YES: the bead split is structurally safe if bead ids_ml_new-opgj.1 exclusively owns base.html/partials/console.css/console.js and downstream surface beads keep their file scopes disjoint.

title: Review Rerun 5 Fix Pass: ids-structured-record-adapter
close_reason: Completed: rerun-5 fix pass cleared review follow-ups and runtime compatibility re-verified

title: Resolve Review P2: Make file-mode sink writes failure-safe
description:
## Problem Statement

In file mode, the adapter can truncate requested output paths before setup or processing fully succeeds. A failed run can therefore leave empty or partial artifacts at the final target paths.

## Evidence

**File:** scripts/ids_record_adapter.py
**Why this is a problem:** Failed runs should not destroy previously valid batch artifacts.

## Proposed Solutions

### Option A — Recommended: stage writes through temp files or equivalent rollback cleanup
**Pros:** Prevents visible truncation until the run succeeds.
**Cons:** Small file-handling refactor.
**Effort:** Small

### Option B — document truncation as current behavior
**Pros:** No code change.
**Cons:** Leaves destructive failure semantics in a supported path.
**Effort:** Small

## Acceptance Criteria

- [ ] If file-mode setup or processing fails before completion, no truncated final output files remain.
- [ ] Regression coverage proves failure cleanup in file mode.
close_reason: Completed: stage file-mode sink writes through temp files and preserve existing outputs on failure

title: Resolve Review P2: Narrow the primary profile to a genuine CICFlowMeter-like source surface
description:
## Problem Statement

The primary profile still accepts a largely canonical 72-feature source shape through identity mappings, which weakens the promised upstream normalization boundary.

## Evidence

**Files:** scripts/ids_record_adapter.py, history/ids-structured-record-adapter/approach.md
**Why this is a problem:** The primary profile should prove actual CICFlowMeter-like normalization rather than acting as a mostly pass-through wrapper.

## Proposed Solutions

### Option A — Recommended: define the primary profile from an explicit upstream alias surface
**Pros:** Aligns implementation with the locked approach guardrail.
**Cons:** Requires tightening accepted source keys and tests.
**Effort:** Medium

### Option B — keep canonical names as accepted aliases and document a compatibility mode
**Pros:** Smaller code change.
**Cons:** Keeps the boundary blurry.
**Effort:** Small

## Acceptance Criteria

- [ ] A bare canonical 72-feature record is not accepted as the primary profile source shape unless explicitly documented as a separate mode.
- [ ] Tests prove the primary profile accepts only intended CICFlowMeter-like aliases plus locked metadata keys.
close_reason: Completed: pin the primary profile to an explicit fixed upstream source surface

title: Resolve Review P2: Cover stdin sink overwrite behavior on reruns
description:
## Problem Statement

Current stdin sink coverage only exercises fresh files. It does not prove that rerunning with pre-existing sink content overwrites rather than appends.

## Evidence

**File:** scripts/ids_record_adapter.py
**Why this is a problem:** Reusing sink files is a realistic workflow for the supported stdin/stdout path.

## Proposed Solutions

### Option A — Recommended: add a regression test with pre-populated sink files
**Pros:** Locks intended overwrite semantics directly.
**Cons:** Adds one CLI regression scenario.
**Effort:** Small

### Option B — rely on current implementation structure only
**Pros:** No new tests.
**Cons:** Leaves rerun semantics vulnerable to silent regression.
**Effort:** Small

## Acceptance Criteria

- [ ] Pre-existing stdin sink files are overwritten, not appended, after a run.
- [ ] A repeat invocation still overwrites cleanly.
close_reason: Completed: prove stdin sink reruns overwrite pre-existing outputs instead of appending

title: Resolve Review P2: Lock down malformed delegated contract result handling
description:
## Problem Statement

The adapter now supports duck-typed delegated contract results, but the malformed-result branch is not covered. A delegated contract returning an object with neither ligned_features nor eason currently has no regression test.

## Evidence

**Files:** scripts/ids_record_adapter.py, 	ests/test_ids_record_adapter.py
**Why this is a problem:** The delegated contract boundary is part of the adapter integration surface and should fail in a stable, intentional way.

## Proposed Solutions

### Option A — Recommended: add a unit test for the malformed delegated result shape
**Pros:** Locks the current failure contract and prevents accidental crash regressions.
**Cons:** Adds one narrow negative-case test.
**Effort:** Small

### Option B — change runtime behavior without tests
**Pros:** None.
**Cons:** Leaves the boundary underspecified.
**Effort:** Small

## Acceptance Criteria

- [ ] A malformed delegated contract result triggers the intended stable TypeError path or another explicitly tested fallback.
- [ ] Regression coverage protects the delegated contract boundary.
close_reason: Completed: lock malformed delegated contract results to the stable TypeError path

title: Resolve Review P3: Cover empty or whitespace-only JSONL inputs
description:
## Problem Statement

Blank-line-only or empty JSONL inputs are currently untested in both file mode and stdin mode.

## Evidence

**File:** scripts/ids_record_adapter.py
**Why this is a problem:** Empty batches are common in streaming workflows and should behave as a clean no-op.

## Proposed Solutions

### Option A — Recommended: add empty-input coverage for both I/O modes
**Pros:** Locks no-op semantics and prevents noisy regressions.
**Cons:** Adds a couple of lightweight tests.
**Effort:** Small

### Option B — leave behavior implicit
**Pros:** No test additions.
**Cons:** No protection against future regressions.
**Effort:** Small

## Acceptance Criteria

- [ ] Empty or whitespace-only inputs produce no adapted records and no unexpected quarantine output.
- [ ] Both file mode and stdin mode exit cleanly on empty input.
close_reason: Completed: cover empty and whitespace-only JSONL inputs in file and stdin modes

title: Resolve Review P2: Unify service config and harden preflight contract
description:
## Problem Statement

The service packaging currently has split-brain runtime configuration and an incomplete preflight boundary. The unit file sets environment variables but hard-codes the same values again in `ExecStart`, and the preflight still does not prove the complete validated dependency contract.

## Evidence

**File:** `deploy/systemd/ids-live-sensor.service`
**Line(s):** environment/preflight/start command sections

- Environment variables are declared but `ExecStart` hard-codes the operational values instead of consuming them.
- Preflight checks do not verify the concrete NIC or exact helper-binary paths/native dependency boundary.
- Security review also flagged that helper binaries are resolved from `PATH` instead of exact configured paths.

**Why this is a problem:**
Deployment drift becomes easy, and the validated Linux boundary is not actually enforced before the daemon starts.

## Proposed Solutions

### Option A — [Recommended] Single source of truth plus exact-path preflight
**Pros:** Reduces config drift, hardens the startup contract, and addresses the PATH trust issue.
**Cons:** Requires tightening both docs and systemd unit examples.
**Effort:** Medium

### Option B — Keep current shape and document the caveat
**Pros:** Minimal implementation work.
**Cons:** Leaves the deployment contract fragile and security-hardening incomplete.
**Effort:** Small

## Acceptance Criteria

- [ ] `ExecStart` derives its runtime values from one source of truth instead of duplicating them.
- [ ] Preflight verifies the configured NIC actually exists.
- [ ] Preflight verifies exact configured helper-binary paths and required native dependencies such as `jnetpcap`.
- [ ] The deployment example no longer relies on bare `PATH` lookups for privileged helper binaries.
close_reason: Completed: service unit now consumes env-driven paths in both ExecStartPre and ExecStart, with exact-path Linux preflight checks

title: Resolve Review P2: Make Settings interactions root-path aware
description:
## Plain-Language Summary

The new Settings feature hardcodes leading-slash URLs for Save, Test, and redirect behavior. That works only when the app is mounted at the site root. In a supported reverse-proxy deployment with a non-empty `root_path`, the page can render but the interactions post outside the mounted app.

## What The Code Does Today

- The settings form posts to `/settings`.
- The client-side Test button fetches `/settings/test`.
- The save handler redirects to `/settings`.
- None of those paths are derived from the request or the configured root path.

## Why This Is A Problem

- The operator console already supports `root_path` and passes it through uvicorn/FastAPI.
- Decision D3 in practice: deploy-readiness work must hold in real Linux deployment shapes, including reverse-proxy mounting.
- This feature adds a new surface that breaks in one of the deployment shapes the console explicitly supports.

## Concrete Failure Scenario

- The console is served behind a proxy at `https://host/console` with `IDS_OPERATOR_CONSOLE_ROOT_PATH=/console`.
- `GET /console/settings` renders the page successfully.
- The admin clicks Save or Test.
- The browser sends the request to `https://host/settings` or `https://host/settings/test` instead of the mounted app path.
- The feature fails even though the page itself loaded.

## Evidence

**File:** `ids/console/templates/settings.html`
**Line(s):** 24-25

The form uses `action=/settings`.

**File:** `ids/console/static/console.js`
**Line(s):** 207-220

The test button fetches `/settings/test`.

**File:** `ids/console/web.py`
**Line(s):** 545

The save handler redirects to `/settings`.

## Proposed Solutions

### Option A — Recommended: make settings URLs root-path aware
**Pros:** keeps the feature working under mounted deployments and matches the app contract.
**Cons:** requires template/JS route plumbing.
**Effort:** Small

### Option B — declare root-path unsupported for Settings
**Pros:** smaller change.
**Cons:** creates a broken exception inside an otherwise root-path-aware app.
**Effort:** Small

## Acceptance Criteria

- [ ] Save, Test, and redirect URLs are generated in a root-path-aware way.
- [ ] The Settings page works when the app is mounted under a non-empty `root_path`.
- [ ] Regression tests cover the mounted-path interaction flow, not just a bare `/settings` route.
notes:
## Phase Context

Phase: Phase 4 - Review closure and release-proof hardening
What Changes: the settings flow works under the supported mounted deployment shape instead of only at site root.
Unlocks Next: the final closure-proof bead can verify the deployed topology without caveats.

## Story Context

Story: Story 3 - Make the settings surface work in real deployed topology and prove closure
What Happens: save, test, and redirect behavior become root-path aware for `/console/...` style deployments.
Contributes To: the exit-state requirement that mounted proxy paths work in the supported topology.
Unlocks: final closure proof and review-ready demonstration.

## Planning Context

From approach.md: mounted-path proof should happen only after the config contract is truthful.

## Institutional Learnings

From history/learnings/20260329-operator-console-production-hardening.md:
- proxy-facing behavior must honor explicit `root_path` rather than assuming leading-slash URLs.

## File Scope

Primary files:
- ids/console/templates/settings.html
- ids/console/static/console.js
- ids/console/web.py
- tests/console/test_ids_operator_console_web.py

## Verification Criteria

- Save, Test, and redirect paths are generated in a root-path-aware way.
- Mounted `/console/settings` interactions work, not just bare `/settings`.
- Regression tests cover mounted-path interaction flow.
close_reason: Completed: settings form, test URL, and redirect all root-path-aware, 4 regression tests cover mounted path

title: Spike: Can the micro-batch runner flush safely under mixed input?
description:
What:
Time-boxed spike to prove whether a JSONL-based micro-batch runner can preserve near-realtime behavior while deterministically flushing valid records, isolating malformed records, and preserving the final partial batch at end-of-stream.

Question to answer:
Can v1 implement a micro-batch runner with only existing Python dependencies such that mixed valid/invalid flow events do not stall the pipeline, and the runner always flushes the last partial batch on shutdown/end-of-stream?

Context:
- This spike corresponds to the HIGH-risk item `Realtime micro-batch runner` in history/ids-pre-model-realtime-pipeline/approach.md.
- Runtime interface for v1 is structured JSONL.
- A YES result must include the operational constraints that make the runner deterministic enough to test.
- A NO result must identify what blocker prevents a safe runner in the current repo constraints.

File scope:
- history/ids-pre-model-realtime-pipeline/approach.md
- history/ids-pre-model-realtime-pipeline/CONTEXT.md
- scripts/ids_realtime_pipeline.py
- scripts/ids_inference.py
- .spikes/ids-pre-model-realtime-pipeline/

Verification criteria:
- Produce FINDINGS.md with a definitive YES or NO.
- The result explicitly addresses malformed-record isolation, flush triggers, and end-of-stream finalization.
close_reason: YES: a JSONL micro-batch runner is feasible with existing Python dependencies if flush triggers are explicit and shutdown drains the final partial batch

title: Resolve Review P2: Keep ids.core.path_defaults runtime-scoped and prove runtime adopters
description:
## Problem Statement

`ids.core.path_defaults` should stay narrowly scoped to product/runtime-facing defaults, and this bead now owns only that runtime/core boundary plus direct runtime adopter proof. The ML packaging topology has been split into `ids_ml_new-d5ae`, so this bead must stop carrying mixed ownership and instead prove the runtime contract cleanly.

## Evidence

**Files:** `ids/core/path_defaults.py`, `ids/runtime/inference.py`, `ids/runtime/adapter/record_adapter.py`, `tests/core/test_ids_path_defaults.py`, `tests/runtime/test_ids_record_adapter.py`, `tests/runtime/test_ids_inference.py`

**Why this is a problem:**
A shared core helper should stay narrow and explicit. If runtime-scoped defaults are not isolated from ML packaging topology, executing agents still have to guess whether runtime and packaging concerns should move together. Validation blocked precisely because that ownership boundary was not explicit enough.

## Proposed Solutions

### Option A — [Recommended] Keep `ids.core.path_defaults` runtime-scoped and prove runtime adopters directly
**Pros:** Restores a cohesive core boundary and keeps runtime consumers on an explicit shared contract.
**Cons:** Requires touching shared defaults plus direct runtime adopter proofs together.
**Effort:** Medium

### Option B — leave runtime/default ownership partially split across modules
**Pros:** Less immediate work.
**Cons:** Preserves boundary drift and weakens the canonical runtime contract.
**Effort:** Small

## Acceptance Criteria

- [ ] `ids.core.path_defaults` exports only runtime/product-facing defaults needed by installed runtime and operator surfaces.
- [ ] Direct runtime adopters of the shared default layer, including `ids.runtime.inference` and `ids.runtime.adapter.record_adapter`, have regression tests proving their effective defaults under both `IDS_REPO_ROOT` override and checkout fallback.
- [ ] The added runtime proofs demonstrate that no runtime adopter silently reintroduces workstation-specific path assumptions.

> KNOWN PATTERN: Matches 20260331-repo-structure-wrapper-contracts.md and earlier ids.core boundary learnings.
> Previous occurrence: `history/learnings/20260331-repo-structure-wrapper-contracts.md`
> Past resolution: keep canonical shared/core modules narrowly scoped and avoid absorbing non-core ownership just because multiple callers happen to need defaults.
notes: Verify: python -m pytest tests/core/test_ids_path_defaults.py tests/runtime/test_ids_record_adapter.py tests/runtime/test_ids_inference.py -q
close_reason: Completed: keep ids.core.path_defaults runtime-scoped and prove runtime adopters

title: Spike: Can canonical installed entrypoints replace wrapper-first operator commands while preserving wrapper compatibility and one operator path?
description:
Validate the entrypoint convergence decision before execution.

Question:
- Can installed canonical entrypoints become the primary operator path in the same feature while existing scripts/* wrappers remain valid compatibility surfaces?

What to prove:
- The canonical module main() surfaces are sufficient to serve as installed CLIs for runtime and ops commands.
- Wrapper compatibility can remain explicit and smoke-testable without staying the primary documented operator path.
- One canonical operator-facing install/bootstrap path can be named without creating a second long-lived story.

YES condition:
- A concrete canonical CLI map exists that preserves wrapper compatibility and supports one operator path.

NO condition:
- The runtime/docs/deploy seams still require wrappers as the primary operator path in phase 1.

Deliverable:
- Write .spikes/ids-repo-installable-full-stack-packaging/<spike-id>/FINDINGS.md with YES or NO and the exact canonical CLI mapping constraints.

Verify:
- Findings enumerate which commands can move to installed entrypoints and which seams, if any, must remain compatibility-only.
close_reason: YES: Installed canonical entrypoints can replace wrapper-first operator commands if deploy/docs move to ids/* CLIs and the operator-console server gains a canonical installed CLI target.

title: IDS Operator Console Production Hardening
description: Production hardening epic for the existing same-host IDS operator console. Scope is limited to deploy safety, secret/config management, reverse proxy/TLS readiness, migration/bootstrap safety, backup/restore/retention, observability, smoke operations, and safe same-host upgrade from v1. Execution must honor history/ids-operator-console-production-hardening/CONTEXT.md and history/ids-operator-console-production-hardening/approach.md, and validating must spike the HIGH-risk items before any code is written.
close_reason: Feature complete: operator console production hardening delivered

title: Harden runtime config for proxy-aware deployment and session security
description:
What:
Harden the running service contract for reverse-proxied same-host production by expanding config loading, server runtime options, session/cookie posture, and layered health/readiness output without breaking the canonical FastAPI app-factory deployment path.

Why:
This bead implements D1, D5, D6, D11, D12, and D15 from CONTEXT.md. The current console still uses placeholder-style env assumptions, https_only=False, and only exposes a minimal /healthz.

How:
- Expand scripts/ids_operator_console/config.py to support proxy-aware runtime fields, explicit public_base_url / root-path settings, and secret-file based loading for sensitive values.
- Extend scripts/ids_operator_console_server.py and scripts/ids_operator_console/web.py so the runtime can safely trust configured proxy headers, generate redirects/cookies against the configured public origin, and use a hardened session-cookie contract.
- Add a richer readiness payload/route surface that distinguishes liveness from config/schema/data-path readiness, while preserving the canonical app factory from web.py.

File Scope:
- scripts/ids_operator_console/config.py
- scripts/ids_operator_console/auth.py
- scripts/ids_operator_console/web.py
- scripts/ids_operator_console/health.py
- scripts/ids_operator_console_server.py
- tests/test_ids_operator_console_config.py
- tests/test_ids_operator_console_auth.py
- tests/test_ids_operator_console_web.py

Verification Criteria:
- python -m py_compile scripts/ids_operator_console_server.py scripts/ids_operator_console/*.py
- python -m pytest -q tests/test_ids_operator_console_config.py tests/test_ids_operator_console_auth.py tests/test_ids_operator_console_web.py
- Add regression coverage proving the runnable entrypoint still serves substantive console routes, that secure cookie behavior fails closed when production secrets/config are invalid, and that the readiness contract reflects proxy/config state correctly.

Context from Planning:
- Approach.md: proxy-aware config + session hardening is a HIGH-risk seam and must stay behind reverse proxy/TLS termination rather than moving TLS into the app.

Institutional Learnings:
- From history/learnings/20260328-operator-console-runtime-wiring.md: keep the service entrypoint wired to the real app factory and verify EXISTS / SUBSTANTIVE / WIRED.
- From history/learnings/20260328-live-sensor-runtime-contracts.md: centralize config and validate exact runtime assumptions rather than relying on duplicated literals or implicit defaults.
notes:
Validated by spike ids_ml_new-gen:
- Add explicit forwarded_allow_ips/trusted-proxy config, optional root_path, and explicit public_base_url.
- Secure cookie posture must fail closed in production (https_only=True, non-placeholder secret, explicit same_site/path/domain handling as needed).
- Keep redirects relative where possible; use public_base_url for any absolute URL contract and smoke-facing origin checks.
- Proxy artifact/preflight must require Host plus X-Forwarded-Proto/X-Forwarded-For forwarding.
close_reason: Completed

title: Implement explicit schema versioning, admin bootstrap, and migration CLI
description:
What:
Add an explicit schema/versioning and bootstrap surface for the operator console so normal startup can verify compatibility and fail closed, while operators get a deliberate path to initialize admin access and migrate from v1.

Why:
This bead implements D7, D8, D9, and part of D15. Discovery found that the current store only bootstraps with CREATE TABLE IF NOT EXISTS, has no schema version metadata, and has no runtime admin bootstrap or password-rotation workflow.

How:
- Add schema metadata and ordered migration primitives in scripts/ids_operator_console/migrations.py, with startup verification hooks in scripts/ids_operator_console/db.py.
- Add an operator CLI foundation in scripts/ids_operator_console_manage.py for bootstrap-admin, migrate, and status/verification commands.
- Keep this bead focused on database/bootstrap and operator-command safety; reuse existing password-hash helpers if needed, but do not expand the runtime session-hardening surface here.

File Scope:
- scripts/ids_operator_console/db.py
- scripts/ids_operator_console/migrations.py
- scripts/ids_operator_console_manage.py
- tests/test_ids_operator_console_db.py
- tests/test_ids_operator_console_ops.py

Verification Criteria:
- python -m py_compile scripts/ids_operator_console_manage.py scripts/ids_operator_console/*.py
- python -m pytest -q tests/test_ids_operator_console_db.py tests/test_ids_operator_console_ops.py
- Add regression coverage proving a v1-style store is rejected by normal startup until an explicit migration command is run, and that bootstrap-admin is explicit rather than implicit.

Context from Planning:
- Approach.md: schema versioning + explicit migration/upgrade path is a HIGH-risk seam that validating must spike before execution starts.

Institutional Learnings:
- From history/learnings/critical-patterns.md: HIGH-risk assumptions need spike beads before swarming.
- From history/learnings/20260328-operator-console-runtime-wiring.md: runtime and operator entrypoints must be reviewed for WIRED behavior, not merely module existence.
notes:
Validated by spike ids_ml_new-n1m:
- Startup must stop mutating schema implicitly; split inspect/verify from bootstrap/migrate.
- Add explicit schema metadata/version tracking and ordered migrations.
- bootstrap-admin and migrate stay in the operator CLI, not request-time runtime helpers.
- Upgrade from v1 is a verify -> explicit migrate -> start path.
close_reason: Completed

title: Add transactional backup, restore, and retention operations
description:
What:
Add transactional backup, restore, and retention operations for operator-owned artifacts using SQLite-safe snapshotting plus manifest-based config/secret-reference tracking, with no unsafe file-copy fallback.

Why:
This bead implements D3, D4, D10, D14, and part of D15. The current console has no backup/restore surface at all, and the feature must prove a restore drill while keeping secret material external to repo-managed artifacts.

How:
- Add scripts/ids_operator_console/ops.py to implement DB backup, restore, manifest creation, retention pruning, and restore validation.
- Extend scripts/ids_operator_console_manage.py with backup, restore, and prune-retention subcommands once the migration CLI foundation exists.
- Ensure backup can run online via SQLite backup primitives, but restore preserves secret references, requires secret material to be re-bound externally, and fails closed unless the console service is stopped and the destination passes offline restore preflight checks.

File Scope:
- scripts/ids_operator_console/ops.py
- scripts/ids_operator_console/db.py
- scripts/ids_operator_console_manage.py

- tests/test_ids_operator_console_db.py

Verification Criteria:

- python -m pytest -q tests/test_ids_operator_console_ops.py tests/test_ids_operator_console_db.py
- Add regression coverage for backup artifact creation, restore of operator-owned state, retention pruning, fail-closed behavior when secret references cannot be satisfied after restore, and refusal to run restore when offline preconditions are not met.

Context from Planning:
- Approach.md: backup/restore workflow + secret-reference restore validation is a HIGH-risk seam.

Institutional Learnings:
- From history/learnings/20260328-adapter-rollback-contract.md: multi-output publish/rollback is one contract, and restore must not rely on copy-based fallback.
- From history/learnings/critical-patterns.md: validating must spike every HIGH-risk restore assumption before execution.
notes:
Validated by spike ids_ml_new-1hs:
- Online backup may use sqlite3 backup primitives against the live WAL database.
- Restore must refuse to run unless offline preconditions are satisfied (service stopped, destination verified, fail-closed on stale/missing secret references).
- Manifest scope must capture config + secret references, never secret material.

title: Wire preflight, systemd packaging, and reverse-proxy artifacts
description:
What:
Wire the hardened runtime contract into deployment artifacts by expanding preflight, systemd packaging, and a concrete reverse-proxy example that is reviewable as a real production topology.

Why:
This bead implements D1, D5, D10, D11, D12, and D13. Discovery showed the current unit still carries placeholder secret config, preflight only validates a narrow contract, and there is no repo artifact proving the console is actually ready to sit behind a reverse proxy.

How:
- Expand scripts/ids_operator_console_preflight.py to validate secret references, schema/admin bootstrap state expectations, and the new runtime contract.
- Harden deploy/systemd/ids-operator-console.service around env-file plus secret-reference usage, keeping same-host internal binding and exact-path startup checks.
- Add one concrete Nginx example under deploy/nginx/ while keeping the broader runtime contract proxy-agnostic.

File Scope:
- scripts/ids_operator_console_preflight.py
- deploy/systemd/ids-operator-console.service
- deploy/nginx/ids-operator-console.conf.example
- tests/test_ids_operator_console_preflight.py
- tests/test_ids_operator_console_notifications.py
- docs/ids_operator_console_architecture.md

Verification Criteria:
- python -m py_compile scripts/ids_operator_console_preflight.py
- python -m pytest -q tests/test_ids_operator_console_preflight.py tests/test_ids_operator_console_notifications.py
- Artifact review must prove the unit, preflight, and proxy example are WIRED to the same runtime contract rather than documenting a topology the code cannot actually support.

Context from Planning:
- Approach.md: systemd/proxy packaging is medium risk but must be wired to the same config/runtime contract as the app, not maintained as a separate fiction.

Institutional Learnings:
- From history/learnings/20260328-live-sensor-runtime-contracts.md: Linux services should use one exact-path config source plus preflight before main startup.
- From history/learnings/20260328-operator-console-runtime-wiring.md: deployment artifacts must satisfy EXISTS / SUBSTANTIVE / WIRED, not only exist in the tree.
notes:
Validated by spikes ids_ml_new-gen and ids_ml_new-1hs:
- Preflight/unit/proxy artifacts must encode trusted proxy headers, root_path/public_base_url expectations, and secure cookie assumptions explicitly.
- Restore-related deployment/runbook artifacts must document and verify offline-only restore discipline and secret-reference revalidation.

title: IDS Operator Console V1
description:
What:
Create the execution container for IDS Operator Console V1, the same-host operator platform that wraps the existing live sensor with backend API, embedded storage, dashboard UX, triage workflow, reporting/export, and Telegram notification support.

Why:
From CONTEXT.md and approach.md: the IDS producer already exists, but the repo still lacks the product layer operators need. This epic tracks the full platform slice while preserving the current IDS-not-IPS boundary.

Acceptance criteria:
- Planning beads exist for foundation, storage, ingest, auth, workflow, UI, reporting, and deployment/notification.
- Validating can inspect explicit write scopes and HIGH-risk items before execution.
close_reason: Feature complete: IDS operator console v1 delivered, review blocker fixed, and full verification passed

title: Scaffold operator console service foundation
description:
What:
Scaffold the operator-console service foundation: package layout, config loading, service entrypoint, and the minimal application bootstrap boundary for a same-host FastAPI/Jinja console.

Why:
From approach.md: every other bead depends on a stable package and app/config boundary. This is the first HIGH-risk introduction of a web/application framework into a repo that is currently script-first only.

Context from Planning:
- Use a Python-native service, not a Node/SPA toolchain.
- Keep the producer (`ids_live_sensor`) separate from the new operator service.
- Same-host deployment and authenticated admin console are locked decisions.

Institutional Learnings:
- From history/learnings/20260328-live-sensor-runtime-contracts.md: same-host services should centralize config and validate exact paths/preconditions instead of scattering deployment assumptions.
- From history/learnings/20260328-adapter-rollback-contract.md: high-risk architectural work should be decomposed into narrow write scopes before multi-agent execution.

File scope:
- scripts/ids_operator_console/__init__.py
- scripts/ids_operator_console/config.py
- scripts/ids_operator_console_server.py
- tests/test_ids_operator_console_config.py

Verification criteria:

- The service entrypoint loads config and builds the minimal application bootstrap without forcing dashboard/business modules to exist yet.
- Focused tests prove config parsing and bootstrap wiring work under repo-root invocation.
notes:
Validated by spike `ids_ml_new-rjq`:
- FastAPI + Jinja is viable in the current repo and local environment without adding a Node/SPA toolchain.
- Keep the foundation bead focused on a Python-native app factory + script-style entrypoint.
- Do not pull dashboard/business modules into the bootstrap contract yet.
close_reason: Completed: scaffolded operator console foundation config, entrypoint, and bootstrap tests

title: Implement operator console SQLite store
description:
What:
Implement the SQLite-backed operator datastore bootstrap and repository primitives needed for alerts, anomalies, summaries, ingest offsets, notes, status history, suppression rules, admin auth state, and notification delivery bookkeeping.

Why:
From approach.md: the product layer cannot stay on raw JSONL alone because operator workflow requires durable relational state. This bead creates the embedded same-host store that all later beads build on.

Context from Planning:
- Storage choice is stdlib `sqlite3`, not a separate DB service.
- The schema must stay sensor-aware for future multi-host expansion while serving one host/sensor now.
- Anomaly handling must preserve redaction-first defaults.
- Telegram delivery state and retry bookkeeping belong in the operator datastore, not in transient process memory.

File scope:
- scripts/ids_operator_console/db.py
- tests/test_ids_operator_console_db.py

Verification criteria:
- DB bootstrap creates all required tables/indexes for the locked v1 workflow.
- Repository primitives cover alerts, anomalies, summaries, ingest offsets, notes/history, suppression, admin state, and notifier delivery state.
- Tests prove bootstrap is repeatable/idempotent and basic CRUD/query flows work against a temp database.
notes:
Validated constraints from spikes `ids_ml_new-kys` and `ids_ml_new-lw1`:
- Persist offset state only after full newline-terminated records parse successfully.
- Track file identity plus offset and reset safely on truncate/replace.
- Include durable notification delivery bookkeeping in the datastore so notifier retries survive restarts.
close_reason: Completed: implemented SQLite operator store bootstrap/repositories with tests

title: Build sensor-output ingest with persisted offsets
description:
What:
Build the same-host ingest pipeline that reads the live sensor's `alerts`, `quarantine`, and `summary` outputs, tracks file identity plus offsets, tolerates partial appended lines, and persists normalized records into the operator datastore.

Why:
From approach.md: this is the core data seam between the existing IDS producer and the new operator platform. If this importer is wrong, the whole console becomes unreliable.

Context from Planning:
- Honor locked decision D8 exactly: ingest from the existing producer outputs first.
- Preserve the semantic split between attack alerts and schema anomalies.
- Suppression must not change producer-side detection or hide anomaly visibility.

Institutional Learnings:
- From history/learnings/20260328-live-sensor-runtime-contracts.md: operator-facing evidence must be durable during runtime, not only on graceful shutdown.
- From history/learnings/20260328-adapter-rollback-contract.md: treat multi-output/restart behavior as one contract and fail closed on unsafe file recovery patterns.

File scope:
- scripts/ids_operator_console/ingest.py
- tests/test_ids_operator_console_ingest.py

Verification criteria:
- The importer can ingest appended sensor JSONL records into SQLite without duplicating previously committed lines.
- Partial trailing lines do not corrupt ingest state.
- Restart/reopen behavior preserves offsets and record integrity.
- Alerts, anomalies, and summaries remain distinct in storage.
notes:
Validated by spike `ids_ml_new-kys`:
- Tail the JSONL files, not journald, because JSONL is the durable source of truth.
- Commit offsets only after full newline-terminated records.
- Detect truncate/replace via file identity plus offset instead of assuming monotonic append forever.
close_reason: Completed: implemented JSONL ingest with persisted offsets and restart/partial/truncate coverage

title: Implement single-admin auth and session boundary
description:
What:
Implement the single-admin auth/session boundary for the operator console, including login handling, password-hash verification/storage usage, signed session cookies, protected routes, and explicit CSRF-aware form behavior appropriate for same-host internal deployment.

Why:
From CONTEXT.md and approach.md: the console must be authenticated even in the one-admin v1 shape, but the repo has no prior auth system. This is a security-critical HIGH-risk bead.

Context from Planning:
- v1 has one internal admin user, not a multi-role system.
- The console is read/triage/monitoring only and should not become a control plane.
- The chosen auth boundary should stay simple enough for same-host deployment.

Institutional Learnings:
- From history/learnings/20260328-adapter-rollback-contract.md: fail closed and preserve safe defaults when handling security-sensitive state.
- From history/learnings/20260328-live-sensor-runtime-contracts.md: deployment values and secret/config paths should be explicit and centrally managed.

File scope:
- scripts/ids_operator_console/auth.py
- tests/test_ids_operator_console_auth.py

Verification criteria:
- Unauthenticated requests to protected console surfaces are rejected/redirected consistently.
- Valid admin login establishes a working signed-cookie session; invalid credentials fail safely.
- Tests cover session persistence, logout, and explicit CSRF-aware form submission behavior.
notes:
Validated by spike `ids_ml_new-rci`:
- Use signed session cookies for the single-admin console.
- Protect state-changing form posts with explicit CSRF tokens.
- Keep protected routes fail-closed for unauthenticated access.
close_reason: Completed: implemented single-admin auth/session helpers with CSRF-protected form workflow and tests

title: Implement alert triage and suppression services
description:
What:
Implement the operator workflow/service layer for alert triage, status transitions, investigation notes, status history, suppression rules, and anomaly-aware query surfaces on top of the datastore.

Why:
From CONTEXT.md: the dashboard must be more than passive viewing. This bead creates the product logic that makes the IDS usable by an operator.

Context from Planning:
- Honor locked workflow states: `new`, `acknowledged`, `investigating`, `resolved`, `false_positive`.
- Alerts are handled independently; no incident/case subsystem in v1.
- Suppression applies to attack-alert presentation/notification only and must not hide schema anomalies.
- Anomalies remain a separate operational lane.

File scope:

- tests/test_ids_operator_console_alerts.py

Verification criteria:
- Alert status transitions persist with history.
- Investigation notes are durable and queryable.
- Suppression rules reduce eligible alert presentation/notification without mutating stored source events.
- Query helpers cleanly separate alerts, anomalies, and summary-derived health views.
notes:
Validated constraints from spikes `ids_ml_new-kys` and `ids_ml_new-lw1`:
- Keep attack alerts, anomalies, and summaries separated exactly as the upstream contract defines them.
- Apply suppression before notification eligibility, but never hide schema anomalies.
- Suppression must not mutate source records or local persistence.

description:
What:
Build the combined-console UI and protected route layer: login page, dashboard overview, alert queue/detail views, anomaly panel, sensor-health/summary surfaces, and minimal sensor-aware JSON endpoints that back the dashboard/API surface using server-rendered templates and static assets.

Why:

title: Implement reporting and export surfaces
description:
What:
Implement reporting and export surfaces for alerts, anomalies, and sensor summaries, preserving operator filters and the redaction-first anomaly posture.

Why:
From locked decision D15: the first user-facing console must support history and export, not just a live queue.

Context from Planning:

title: Add Telegram notifications and console deployment packaging
description:
What:
Add Telegram notification delivery plus same-host deployment packaging for the operator console, including notifier failure isolation, durable retry bookkeeping, exact-path preflight, service unit wiring, and architecture documentation.

Why:
From locked decisions D12/D13/D20: v1 needs at least one proactive outbound channel and a real same-host deployment path, but neither should destabilize ingest or triage.

Context from Planning:
- Telegram is the first outbound channel.
- Notification failures must not block local persistence or operator workflow.
- Telegram sending should happen after local state is durable and should honor retry/backoff semantics.
- Same-host deployment should mirror the sensor's config/preflight discipline.

Institutional Learnings:
- From history/learnings/20260328-live-sensor-runtime-contracts.md: use one config source plus exact-path preflight for same-host services.
- From history/learnings/20260328-live-sensor-runtime-contracts.md: operator-facing evidence must remain durable even if downstream notification delivery fails.

File scope:
- scripts/ids_operator_console/notifications.py
- scripts/ids_operator_console_preflight.py
- docs/ids_operator_console_architecture.md
- deploy/systemd/ids-operator-console.service
- tests/test_ids_operator_console_notifications.py

Verification criteria:
- Telegram notifier behavior is isolated from ingest/triage persistence failures and records durable retry/backoff state.
- Preflight validates the console's exact-path config/runtime assumptions before systemd starts the service.
- Tests or dry-run startup coverage demonstrate notifier failure does not break local operator state.
notes:
Validated by spike `ids_ml_new-lw1`:
- Local persistence/triage state must commit before Telegram delivery attempts.
- Telegram sending should run in a decoupled notifier path with durable retry/backoff bookkeeping.
- Notifier failure must never hide or roll back the local operator record.
close_reason: Completed: Telegram notifications, operator preflight, service unit, architecture docs, and tests

title: Resolve Review P1: Wire operator service entrypoint to full console app
description:
## Problem Statement

The shipped service entrypoint does not mount the actual operator console application implemented in `scripts/ids_operator_console/web.py`. `scripts/ids_operator_console_server.py` still builds a bootstrap-only FastAPI app with just `/healthz` and a root template response, so the authenticated dashboard routes, alert detail pages, anomaly/report pages, and JSON endpoints are not reachable when the systemd service starts.

This breaks the production feature slice itself: the code for the combined console exists and is substantive, but the service that operators are instructed to run never exposes it. In practice this means the feature cannot satisfy D1, D4, D5, D14, or the planned dashboard/API surface at runtime.

## Evidence

**File:** `scripts/ids_operator_console_server.py`
**Line(s):** 18-53

```python
from scripts.ids_operator_console import OperatorConsoleConfig, load_operator_console_config

def build_operator_console_app(config: OperatorConsoleConfig) -> FastAPI:
    config.ensure_runtime_dirs()
    templates = Jinja2Templates(directory=str(config.templates_dir))

    app = FastAPI(title="IDS Operator Console", version="0.1.0")
    ...
    @app.get("/healthz", response_class=JSONResponse)
    def healthz() -> dict[str, object]:
        ...

    @app.get("/", response_class=HTMLResponse)
    def index(request: Request) -> HTMLResponse:
        ...
    return app
```

**Related file:** `scripts/ids_operator_console/web.py`
**Line(s):** 94-390

```python
def create_operator_console_web_app(config: OperatorConsoleConfig, *, store: OperatorStore | None = None) -> FastAPI:
    ...
    @app.get("/dashboard", response_class=HTMLResponse)
    def dashboard(...):
        ...

    @app.get("/alerts/{alert_id}", response_class=HTMLResponse)
    def alert_detail(...):
        ...

    @app.get("/api/v1/console/snapshot", response_class=JSONResponse)
    def api_console_snapshot(...):
        ...
```

**Why this is a problem:** the real console routes are implemented but never integrated into the runnable service path, so the deployment contract points at the wrong app.

## Proposed Solutions

### Option A - [Recommended] Replace bootstrap app construction with the real web app factory
**Pros:** fixes the actual runtime surface with the least conceptual churn; keeps one canonical app wiring path.
**Cons:** may require moving `/healthz` into `web.py` or composing it there.
**Effort:** Small

### Option B - Mount the web app inside the bootstrap server wrapper and keep `build_operator_console_app`
**Pros:** preserves the current entrypoint shape if there is a reason to keep a wrapper.
**Cons:** adds an unnecessary second wiring layer and more room for drift.
**Effort:** Medium

## Acceptance Criteria

- [ ] `scripts/ids_operator_console_server.py` runs the same authenticated console app that defines `/dashboard`, `/alerts/{id}`, `/anomalies`, `/reports`, and `/api/v1/*` routes.
- [ ] The service entrypoint still exposes a health endpoint without maintaining a separate bootstrap-only app.
- [ ] A verification test proves the runnable server/app factory exposes the combined-console routes, not only a placeholder root page.
close_reason: Completed: wired service entrypoint to full console app and added regression coverage

title: Final Review Residual Cleanup

title: Restore a neutral read-only bundle status seam and preserve compatibility re-exports
description:

title: Strengthen restore authenticity and complete restore-guard coverage
description:
## Problem Statement

Restore integrity still trusts manifest metadata stored inside the attacker-editable backup payload itself, and the new restore guards still lack coverage for blank-digest and pre-swap schema-validation failures.

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Restore integrity is verified against a trusted source outside the backup payload itself.
- [ ] Blank/missing digest manifests fail closed.
- [ ] Non-current schema backups fail before live DB replacement.
- [ ] Security-focused restore tests cover the final contract.

title: Add docs-lane smoke and unify wrapper smoke harness
description:
## Problem Statement

The docs-lane cleanup still lacks automated smoke for compatibility stubs and mirrored quick-test paths, and the new wrapper smoke harness is duplicated across multiple test modules.

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Automated docs/path smoke validates the new compatibility stubs and documented mirrored pytest commands.
- [ ] Wrapper smoke checks reuse a shared helper or equivalent single harness to reduce drift.
- [ ] Focused docs/wrapper smoke tests pass.
